observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE instruct the Inventory Committee to follow the guidelines and procedures as provided for in COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 in the conduct of physical count of PPE for the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances of the Municipality to establish the existence, condition and accuracy of PPE account balances. Moreover, require them to derecognize all tangible items below the capitalization threshold and follow the prescribed procedures in the disposition of unserviceable properties in order not to overstate the PPE and related accounts and to ascertain the fair presentation of the same in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Management was able to conduct the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances. However, they did not comply with the step-by-step procedures outlined in COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE direct the Municipal Accountant to exert extra effort to locate records of these receivables and to payables. If such efforts exerted are futile, apply then the procedures prescribed under Item 7.0 of COA Circular 2023-008, dated August 17, 2023. Likewise, revert the undocumented payables to the unappropriated surplus of the general fund in conformity with Section 98 of PD 1445.
observations.reason: The recommendation was not complied with by management due non-availability of accurate records/subsidiary ledgers on the breakdown of the debtors.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE require the OIC- Municipal Treasurer to maintain Real Property Tax Register/Taxpayer’s Index Card to identify the list of taxpayers and uncollected amounts that would support the established receivables from real property taxes and to monitor tax collections and delinquencies. Also, instruct him to furnish the Municipal Accountant a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year as the basis of the latter in recording RPT and SET Receivables at the beginning of every year.
observations.reason: The audit recommendation was not complied with by management.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE through the BAC to strictly adhere to the pertinent provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of the RA No. 9184 on the posting requirements of bid opportunities and notices to ensure transparency and accountability in the procurement process.
observations.reason: The recommendation was not fully complied with by management. Reiterated in Observation No. 4, pages 40 to 42 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE instruct the BAC to revisit the pertinent provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184 and recommend the appropriate mode of procurement, thus the legality, validity and propriety of the transactions could be ascertained.
observations.reason: The recommendation was not fully complied with by management. Reiterated in Observation No. 10, pages 52 to 54 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Accountant to cause the immediate submission of the lacking documents and justifications on the deficiencies observed for further evaluation of the transactions and to avoid suspension in audit. Also, oblige the Municipal Accountant to see to it that disbursement vouchers are duly supported with the required basic documents to ensure their validity and propriety.
observations.reason: The recommendation was not fully complied with by management.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE enforce the collection of these taxes, and: a. require the Municipal Treasurer to exert more efforts to collect from the taxpayers and for those who have been unable to pay their taxes for several years, issue notices of delinquency for them to settle their unpaid taxes; and b. if circumstances so warrant, avail of the Civil remedies provided for by the provisions of the Local Government Code (RA 7160), specifically Sections 174 to 176 thereof. Section 174 provides for the civil remedies which are by (a) administrative action through distraint of personal properties and (b) by judicial actions. Section 175 provides for the procedure on the distraint of personal property and Section 176 provides for the levy of Real Properties.
observations.managementAction: The audit recommendation was not fully complied with by management. However, the management issued Notices of Tax Delinquency to the concerned taxpayers.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE require the Implementing Unit the prompt implementation of all programs/projects funded out of the tobacco excise tax to give the constituents, especially the tobacco farmers, the early benefits that could be derived therefrom.
observations.reason: The recommendation was not complied with by management. Reiterated in Observation No. 10, pages 52 to 54 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE enforce the immediate settlement of the audit suspensions as prescribed in the RRSA under COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009. Moreover, require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that supporting documents of transactions are complete, proper and valid in order to prevent the same from being suspended/ disallowed in post-audit.
observations.managementAction: The recommendation was not complied with by management.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Observation No. 11, pages 54 to 55 of this report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that: 

The Municipal Treasurer (MT) furnish the Municipal Accountant (MA) a duly certified list showing the name of the tax payers and the amount due; 

The MA to prepare adjusting entries to record prior years’ delinquent receivable and set-up at the beginning of the year the current year’s receivable for RPT and SET equivalent to 1 percent of the total assessed value of the real property; and 

Strict compliance to Section 246 of R.A. No. 7160 or the Local Government Code and Section 20 of the Manual on the NGAS, Volume 1 for LGUs for fair presentation of receivables and deferred income accounts in the financial statements of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:

Analyze the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds accounts, trace back differences and the nature of the accounts;

Prepare the necessary adjusting entries to bring the correct balances of the accounts; and

Maintain subsidiary ledger as necessary and include disclosure in the Notes to FS for fair presentation of Financial Statements.
observations.reason: The difference is due to accumulated omissions or accounting errors from prior years. Validation and review are still ongoing.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that Management:

Conduct actual annual inventory count of all supplies on stock by the Municipality as at year-end; and

Prioritize the maintenance of stock cards and supplies ledger cards and the reconciliation of balances with the accounting records. Prepare adjusting entries, if any, to reflect the correct balance of the affected accounts.
observations.reason: The Municipality does not have a designated stockroom for supplies. As supplies were issued directly to the end-users, the remaining supplies of the Municipality are distributed among various offices, making it difficult to conduct a comprehensive and complete physical count of all remaining supplies.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the Inventory Committee to conduct an annual and complete physical inventory of PPEs and immediately reconcile the results thereof with the accounting records to permit fair presentation of the Property, Plant and Equipment account in the Statement of Financial Position. Likewise, the Accounting Office and General Services Office to prepare and continuously maintain PPE ledger cards and property cards, respectively.
observations.reason: The physical count was ordered to be conducted on a per-office basis; however, it was not properly managed or monitored. As a result, the completeness of the physical inventory of all PPEs could not be confirmed. Additionally, issues encountered during the consolidation process prevented the finalization of the report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management: 

Continuously require/demand the immediate settlement of the remaining overdue cash advances and if necessary, withhold the salaries of the concerned officials and employees for the settlement thereof; and

Strictly comply with the provisions of Section 89 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 97 – 002 dated February 10, 1997 in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.
observations.reason: Some of the Accountable Officers are either deceased, retired or already separated from the Municipality.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management direct:

The MT, to not allow delayed or partial remittances of collections from RCCs;

The MA, to see to it that all collections and remittances are recorded on appropriate time or year; and

All collectors concerned, to exercise due diligence in the collection, remittance and safekeeping otherwise, impose sanctions against erring collectors.
observations.reason: Some collectors still have unremitted balances.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

Expedite the implementation of all on-going programmed and funded projects under the 20% DF to optimize utilization thereof and to ensure timely delivery of intended benefits for the constituents of the Municipality;

Continuously ensure that the development projects that may be included under the 20% DF shall be those that are necessary, appropriate, or incidental to efficient and effective local governance, and those which are essential to the promotion of the general welfare of the people; and

Ensure that the development projects to be funded out of the 20% DF are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready.
observations.managementAction: The Management holds quarterly meetings to evaluate the appropriateness and implementation of PPAs under 20%
observations.reason: The projects and activities programmed under the 20% Development Fund were not fully implemented in CY 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management strictly enforce the COA Orders of Execution (COE) by requiring the Municipal Treasurer to withhold salaries or other compensation due to the persons liable in satisfaction of the disallowed transactions in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-006.
observations.reason: Some liable persons were either deceased, retired or separated from the agency.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management, through the GSO, require each office to prepare and submit their Monthly Report of Fuel Consumptions for consolidation and for the driver of each of the motor vehicle of the Municipality to prepare at the end of the month. A Monthly Report of Official Travels, summarizing in chronological order his trips for the month which shall be certified by the concerned driver and approved by the Municipal Mayor or his/her authorized representative to ensure that fuel consumption is effectively monitored and used only for official businesses in conformity with COA Circular 77-61.
observations.reason: Some employees had not yet acquired the necessary expertise to prepare the required documents.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management stop the practice of allowing reimbursements to officials and employees and enforce direct payment to suppliers or creditors as prescribed under Section 93 of PD 1445 and RA9184.
observations.reason: Certain transactions in CY 2024 were processed through reimbursements rather than setting up a Petty Cash Fund for small and emergency expenses.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, thru the Bids and Awards Committee, strictly monitor compliance by the BAC Secretariat of its functions under R.A. No. 9184 and its IRR to ensure that the necessary procurement activities are undertaken and that documentary requirements are properly accomplished, and submitted to the concerned offices, put index tabs/tabbings on the documents to be submitted to facilitate checking thereof.
observations.reason: Submission of contracts, their supporting documents, and Purchase Orders to the Office of the Auditor was delayed. Additionally, some contracts lacked complete supporting documents.

observations.managementAction: The Management has started to reconcile its records between the Office of the Municipal Engineer and the Office of the Municipal Accountant to be able to comply with the recommendations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management strictly adhere to the provisions of Circular No. 2009-006 regarding the submission of accounts for a timely review, verification and communication of audit results.
observations.managementAction: The Management did not timely submit the reports.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management instruct:

The Municipal Treasurer, to not allow delayed or partial remittances of collections from RCCs;

The Municipal Accountant, to see to it that all collections and remittances are recorded on appropriate time or year; and

All Accountable Officers (AOs) concerned, to exercise due diligence in the collection, remittance and safekeeping otherwise, impose sanctions against erring AOs.
observations.managementAction: All collecting officers regularly remit their collections and Management only issues new Accountable forms to the Accountable officers if the collections were all remitted. However, there is a substantial amount unremitted at year end.

The Management exerts effort to comply with the recommendations but finds it difficult to complete the adjusting entries because of the absence of proper turnover from previous employees who handled the accounts. The Management committed to inquire with the banks to help in determining the unidentified reconciling items.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following to the Management to enhance the reliability of related accounts in the financial statements and safeguard government funds from possible misuse or misappropriation:

demand immediate and full liquidation of all cash advances of officers and employees that were already deemed accomplished or completed and if no action is taken by them, consider withholding their salaries, if warranted;
refrain from granting additional cash advances to officials and employees with previous unliquidated balances; and
adhere strictly to the provision of Section 89 of PD 1445 and follow the rules and regulations on the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances provided in COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.
observations.managementAction: The Management issued demand letters to concerned employees and committed to refrain from granting additional cash advances to officers and employess with unliquidated cash advances.

The Management issued various demand letters to employees with existing unliquidated cash advances and ensure that no additional cash advances will be given to those who have unliquidated cash advances. However, there are still employees who have not fully liquidated their cash advances.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require:

The Inventory Committee to submit the RPCI and henceforth prepare and submit yearly to the Auditor a duly certified and approved RPCI not later than January 31 of each year; and

The Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting entry if there’s any to reflect the actual balance of Inventories as at December 31, 2023 for fair presentation of the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The Management committed to create an inventory committee to be able to comply with the recommendations.

strictly comply with the provisions of Paragraph 27 of IPSAS 1 to ensure the accuracy and reliability of the account presented in the financial statements.

The Municipal Treasurer and Accountant to maintain Property Cards and Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards respectively and observe proper maintenance and regular monitoring thereof; and

The Accounting and Property Office to analyze and reconcile the accounting records and property records as soon as the RPCPPE are prepared for a fair presentation of the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The responsible officers are still in the process of reconciling their records and the Management is in the process of updating their records to conduct actual physical count due to absence of records of PPEs in the previous years.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following:
	
Direct the Inventory Committee to conduct the semestral, physical count of inventory items by type, and prepare the Report of Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI). Thereafter, submit a copy to the Auditor, pursuant to Section 124 of the MNGAS for LGUs;
Instruct the General Services Office to consolidate weekly the Requisition Issue Slip using the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued. Based on the SSMI, a JEV shall be prepared by the Municipal Accountant pursuant to Section 121 of the MNGAS for LGUs; and

Prepare the necessary adjustment to reflect the actual correct balances thereof. Likewise, both records of the Municipal Accountant’s Office and General Services Office should be adjusted accordingly in order to present fairly the financial position of the Municipality.

in the future, for transactions involving receivables, the Municipal Accountant should maintain subsidiary ledgers in accordance with Section 4 (e) of the NGAS to ensure complete information on the accounts and to facilitate the collection thereof.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Accountant to trace the original entry made and make the necessary adjustments on the outstanding payable accounts and ensure that such claims are properly documented and supported with valid claims of speciﬁc creditors, processed vouchers or payrolls, demand payments or bills. Likewise, the Accounting Unit should maintain complete and updated subsidiary ledgers for Payable accounts. The payable accounts that have been outstanding for more than 2 years or more with no documents evidencing valid claims should be reverted to unappropriated surplus of the general fund.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management: 

Continuously require/demand the immediate settlement of the remaining overdue cash advances and if necessary, withhold the salaries of the concerned officials and employees for the settlement thereof; and

Strictly comply with the provisions of Section 89 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 97 – 002 dated February 10, 1997 in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.

Also, some AOs are still being issued additional cash advances despite not having liquidated their previous ones. There were also employees who are not designated as Disbursing Ofiicers but granted with cash advances for expenses other than for travel.

observations.recommendation:
We commended the Management for the high utilization rate of the fund. However, recommended that Management:

Expedite the implementation of all on-going programmed and funded projects under DF to optimize utilization thereof and to ensure timely delivery of intended benefits for the constituents of the Municipality;

Continuously ensure that the development projects that may be included under DF shall be those that are necessary, appropriate, or incidental to efficient and effective local governance, and those which are essential to the promotion of the general welfare of the people; and

Ensure that the development projects to be funded out of DF are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready.
observations.reason: The weather conditions (rainy season) in the Municipality and delays in the issuance of permits, as most of the project locations are situated within areas covered by Certificates of Ancestral Domain Title (CADT) or the Quirino Protected Landscape (QPL), made the implementation of some projects challenging.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Management through the Local School Board, being the one responsible in the formulation, approval, and utilization of the SEF Budget as provided under RA No. 7160 or the Local Government Code to:

Continuously sustain its efforts in ensuring that all budgeted projects, programs and activities are optimally and fully implemented; 

Prioritize and allocate more funds for the construction, repair and maintenance of school buildings and other facilities of public schools; and

From time to time, revisit the revised guidelines on the use of SEF under DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017 to ensure a more strategic and efficient formulation and utilization of resources for priority programs and projects.
observations.reason: There are still some PPAs that have not been implemented.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management review the reported Due to NGAs in the Financial Statements, check the implementation of the PPAs under this account, cause the liquidation thereof and return any unused/unutilized balance to the SA.

observations.recommendation:
1.4 We recommended that the LCE instruct the following:

a. The Municipal Accountant to reinstate the derecognized PPE items in the accounting records pending the grant of authority for derecognition by the COA;

b. The Municipal Accountant to establish and recognize receivables for missing PPEs attributable to accountable officers/personnel, based on depreciated replacement cost; and

c. The Municipal Accountant to ensure that the required disclosures related to missing and derecognized PPEs are properly included in the Notes to Financial Statements in accordance with Section 9.2 of COA Circular No. 2020-006.

1.5 The Municipal Accountant agreed to comply with the audit recommendations.

2.6 We recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Treasurer to release the RPT shares of the Barangays within the prescribed period of five (5) days after the end of each quarter regardless of the amount as set forth in Section 271 (d) of RA No. 7160 to provide additional funds for the Barangays in the implementation of programs/projects/activities that would benefit their constituents.

2.7 The Municipal Treasurer informed the audit team that he was unaware of the rules and regulations in the release of RPT shares of the Barangays. Further, he agreed to comply with the audit recommendation.

3.7 We recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Treasurer to deposit the collections intact daily or as soon as it reaches the threshold as prescribed by Section 32, Volume I of the MNGAS for LGUs and Section 28 of COA Circular No. 92-382 dated July 3, 1992 in order to avoid possible loss or misuse of the undeposited collection and enables the Municipality to earn interest that could accrue from these collections.

3.8 The Municipal Treasurer stated that the said undeposited collections have been deposited on the early part of the succeeding year. He assured the audit team that he will strictly monitor the timely remittance and deposit of collections. He further agreed to comply with the audit recommendation to avoid delay in the deposit of collections.

4.9 We recommended that the LCE through the BAC to strictly adhere on the posting requirements of bid opportunities and notices, now governed by pertinent provisions of the IRR of RA No. 12009, to ensure transparency and accountability in the procurement process.

4.10 The BAC Secretariat informed the audit team that the unstable internet connection caused the late posting of procurement opportunities and notices. Nonetheless, the Secretariat agreed to strictly comply with the audit recommendation.

5.6 We recommended that the LCE, through the BAC, to require the winning bidder to post the performance security prior to contract signing. In addition, require the BAC through the BAC Technical Working Group (TWG) to ensure that performance securities are dated, with validity period and callable on demand, thus enable the municipality to determine the accountability period of the contractor, hence enforce performance of its obligation and the potential loss of government funds would be avoided.

5.7 The management agreed to comply with the audit recommendation.

6.7 We recommended that the LCE instruct the Implementing Office to observe closer monitoring and evaluation of all ongoing infrastructure projects to ensure the completion of the projects within the specified contract duration. Also, implement the calibrated measures for contracts with negative slippage during the contract duration as provided under GPPB Circular No. 03-2019 dated March 8, 2019, and Rule XXI of the new IRR of RA No.12009, to ensure the timely implementation of the infrastructure projects.

6.8 The management agreed to comply with the audit recommendation. The Implementing Office will send notices to erring contractors to strictly follow the contract as agreed upon, unless with justifiable reasons to warrant the issuance of Suspension Order.

7.7 We recommended that the LCE instruct the HRMO to prepare the payroll for the grant of Subsistence Allowance of Health and Social Workers based on the actual services rendered to ascertain the accuracy and validity of claims and to avoid disallowance in audit.

7.8 The management agreed to comply with the audit recommendation. Further, they assured the audit team that they will specifically set a monthly cut-off period for them to properly compute the subsistence allowances due to the Health and Social Workers.

8.5 We recommended that the LCE instruct the Implementing Office to observe closer monitoring and evaluation of all ongoing infrastructure projects to ensure the implementation of the projects within the approved designs/plans, specifications and specified contract duration.

9.9 We recommended that the LCE enforce the collection of these taxes, and:
a. Require the Municipal Treasurer to exert more efforts to collect from the taxpayers and for those who have been unable to pay their taxes for several years, issue notices of delinquency for them to settle their unpaid taxes; and
b. If circumstances so warrant, avail of the Civil remedies provided for by the provisions of the Local Government Code (RA 7160), specifically Sections 174 to 176 thereof. Section 174 provides for the civil remedies which are by (a) administrative action through distraint of personal properties and (b) by judicial actions. Section 175 provides for the procedure on the distraint of personal property and Section 176 provides for the levy of Real Properties.

9.10 The Municipal Treasurer stated that notices have been issued to the delinquent taxpayers and he is currently updating the RPTAR for the deletion of duplicate collectibles to ensure accuracy of records. In addition, he disclosed that a portion of the receivables pertains to assessed tax due from ISECO and he will secure their tax exemption for the removal of their tax liability. Further, he agreed to comply with the audit recommendations.

10.10 We recommended that the LCE require the prompt implementation of all projects/programs funded out of the tobacco excise tax in order to give the constituents, especially the tobacco farmers the early benefits that could be derived therefrom. We further recommended that the LCE request the Sangguniang Bayan to prepare resolution for the reappropriation of the savings or unused funds in accordance with the authorized uses as prescribed in Republic Act Nos. 7171 and 8240. Ensure that only feasible programs/projects/activities are included in the Annual Investment Program and require regular monitoring of implementation as basis in taking appropriate action. Thus, the benefits that could be derived from the programs/project would be timely and judiciously extended to the tobacco farmers, as they are the very reason for which the funds were created.

10.11 The Municipal Engineer stated that the projects Continuation of Poblacion Farmers Multi-Purpose Building and Continuation of Peg-an Flood Control Poblacion have been implemented on the early part of the succeeding year, while the rest are for implementation. Further, they agreed to comply with the audit recommendations.

11.5 We recommended that the LCE enforce the immediate settlement of the audit suspensions as prescribed in the RRSA under COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009. Moreover, require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that supporting documents of transactions are complete, proper and valid in order to prevent the same from being suspended/ disallowed in post-audit.

11.6 The management informed the audit team that they will start processing the titling of the purchased lot. The Municipal Accountant further agreed to comply with the audit recommendation.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant continue her effort in complying the timely preparation of Bank Reconciliation Statements for fair presentation of Cash in Bank account of the Municipality.
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant recognize impairment allowance on dormant and uncollectible accounts.

We reiterated our recommendation that management act assertively on the disallowance issued and enforce the immediate refund of such disallowance considering that loss of government funds was already incurred. Tolerating delayed refunds would further contribute to additional loss of government funds.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Local Chief Executive:

Advise the Municipal Agriculture Officer to provide the inventory of breeding stocks and other biological assets, if any and reconcile with the Municipal Accountant on the actual number of cows/large cattle owned by the Municipality of Quirino.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
advise the LSB to submit a copy of the DepEd duly approved School Improvement Plan (SIP) and the Division Education Development Plan (DEDP) for the Audit Team to determine whether the procurement of the said vehicle is included in the approved SIP and DEDP for CY 2024;
advise the LSB to submit a copy of the three-year program indicating strategic prioritization policies in the allocation of the SEF; and
submit the legal basis for utilizing the SEF as payment on the procurement one (1) brand-new van amounting to ₱2,387,330.00

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer rectify the scaling noted on the above-mentioned project to avoid further damage of the pavement and communicate to the Audit Team upon completion for evaluation purposes.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer rectify the scaling and traversal cracks noted on the above-mentioned projects to avoid further damage of the pavement and communicate to the Audit Team upon completion for evaluation purposes. Likewise, require JMU Construction to pay the deficiency noted amounting to ₱44,121.12.

We recommended that the Municipality stop the practice of payments of gas expenses for the rehabilitation of barangay road projects thru Petty Cash Fund but instead should draw checks directly in the name of suppliers or creditors in compliance with provisions of Section 93 of Presidential Decree No. 1445 and item 2.2 of COA Circular 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.

We reiterated our recommendation that the:
Local Chief Executive ensure the optimum utilization of the 20% DF by promptly implementing the priority development projects as reflected in its duly approved local development plans and establish the effective and efficient implementation of the projects to maximize the developmental benefits for the intended beneficiaries;
MPDC (i) initiate close coordination with the Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Accountant for the proper monitoring of project implementation and the provision of all the necessary information in the Status Report of Government PPAs before submission to the Audit Team; and
Municipal Engineer (i) require timely implementation of PPAs in coordination with the MPDC under their responsibility at all times.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer furnish the Municipal Accountant with the original Disbursement Vouchers (DV) and its supporting documents, for the preparation of the Journal Entry Voucher following the procedures prescribed to ensure recording of such cancellation. Secure the addresses of the payees for notification of the return of stale checks in their possession for replacement.

We recommended that Management through the Municipal Accountant to conduct verification, analysis, and validation of the existence of Due from Other LGUs and determine the concerned debtors to ascertain their collectability. Likewise, develop and enforce well-defined procedures for collection of receivables to avoid the accumulation of overdue receivables and the deprivation of potential government income.

We recommended that the Management:
a. Require the Supply Officer to keep stock cards for each inventory item and the Accounting Office to maintain supply ledger cards;
b. Direct the Inventory Committee to conduct a physical count of inventory items every six months, prepare a Report of Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI), and submit a copy to the Auditor, following Section 124 of the MNGAS for LGUs; and
c. Instruct the Supply Officer to consolidate weekly Requisition Issue Slips into the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI). Based on the SSMI, the Municipal Accountant will prepare a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to reflect the correct inventory balances, as required by Section 121 of the MNGAS for LGUs.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Inventory Committee to conduct an annual and complete physical inventory of PPEs and immediately reconcile the results thereof with the accounting records to permit fair presentation of the account in the Statement of Financial Position;
Instruct the Municipal Treasurer in the absence of the General Services Officer to prepare and maintain property cards; and
After reconciliation, consider disposing the unserviceable PPEs, request from COA for the derecognition of non-existing and missing PPEs from the Agency’s book of accounts, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020, otherwise known as the Guidelines and Procedures in the Conduct of Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE), Recognition of PPE Items Found at Station, and Disposition for Non-existing/Missing PPE Items, for the One-Time Cleansing of PPE Account Balances of Government Agencies.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure the timely preparation and submission of the General Ledger (GL) and Subsidiary Ledgers (SL) in accordance with established rules and regulations. Regular updates and reviews should be conducted to ensure that all financial records are complete, accurate, and up to date, thereby minimizing delays in the audit process and enhancing the transparency and accountability of the Municipality’s financial management.

We recommended that the Management:
Submit a written explanation for not transferring the whole amount of the unexpended balance of the DRRMF to the STF; and
Transfer the unexpended balance of DRRMF, excluding amounts appropriated for capital outlays, to the Trust Fund and record it as Trust Liabilities – DRRMF and adhere to the guidelines specified under Items 5.1.10 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.

We recommended that Management through the Municipal Accountant:
Properly record all financial transactions using the appropriate account titles based on the nature of transactions in conformity with the revised chart of accounts in order to permit Management to review its activities according to areas of responsibilities and allow for a clearer and fair presentation of transactions in the financial statements of the municipality; and
Prepare the Journal Entry Voucher to reclassify all accounts currently charged to Other Maintenance and Operating Expenses (Other MOOE) into the most appropriate accounts, following the prescribed Chart of Accounts.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to identify the variance, reconcile with BIR and prepare the necessary adjusting entries to reflect the accurate balance; and
Ensure the timely remittance of withheld taxes within the prescribed period to comply with regulatory requirements and avoid penalties.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to determine the root cause of the variance, reconcile historical balances with the respective agencies and prepare the necessary adjusting entries to maintain accurate liability reporting; and
Ensure timely remittance of premium contributions within the prescribed period to maintain compliance with regulatory requirements and avoid penalties.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the concerned personnel to submit the lacking documents and ensure they are properly tabbed/indexed for quick reference during evaluation; and
Designate responsible personnel to oversee, facilitate, and monitor the timely submission of all perfected contracts, purchase orders, and supporting documents in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-001.

We recommended that Management, through the Municipal DRRM Officer, utilize the LDRRMF strictly for implementation of projects and activities exclusively for disaster prevention, mitigation, preparedness, response, rehabilitation and recovery as envisioned in NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC JMC No. 2014-01, Section 5 of NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 and COA Circular No. 2012-002.
Furthermore, we recommended that Management, through the Municipal Accountant, review the supporting documents and charges against the LDRRMF before preparation of Disbursement Vouchers.

We recommended that the Local School Board, thru its Secretariat, ensures that all relevant officials, including the Local Budget Officer, Local Accountant, and Local Treasurer, receive a copy of the SEF Budget within the stipulated 7-day period from its approval to avoid delay in the monitoring and control of the SEF.

We recommended that Management:
Analyze the reasons for the delay in submitting the Financial Statements and develop strategies to address them;
Improve internal controls and financial reporting procedures by establishing a Financial Reporting Manual with clear guidelines, deadlines, and responsibilities; and
Designate specific staff members to oversee and enforce adherence to submission deadlines.

We recommended that Management through the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to reconcile the noted difference of ₱89,877.69 existing on the two receivable accounts and furnish results of reconciliation or adjusting entries made to the Office of the Auditor.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant record the parcels of land owned by the Municipality acquired through donations following the provisions of IPSAS 17 and to provide detailed schedule on the composition of the account for verification, monitoring and reconciliation purposes.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:

Exert earnest efforts in locating the documents and records of prior years, analyze the same to effect necessary adjustments, and come up with a more accurate and reliable financial information.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer replace the stale checks if returned by creditors; or if not, revert the amount to payable accounts. Likewise, the Municipal Accountant should prepare the necessary adjusting entries to reflect reliable balance of the cash and payable accounts in the financial statements of the Municipality.

We recommended that Management through the Municipal Accountant to locate the pertinent documents supporting such claims to ascertain their collectability and enforce its settlement. If proven to be no longer collectible and settlement is deemed remote, request for authority to write-off dormant receivable accounts and attach required documents prescribed under COA Circular No. 2023-008.

The Municipal Accountant to restore the amounts dropped and follow strictly follow the procedures set forth in COA Circular No. 2020-006 in the derecognition/dropping of LGU properties from the books of accounts.

We reiterated our recommendations that Management through the Municipal Accountant:
Trace the original entry made and make the necessary adjustments on the outstanding payable accounts;
Ensure that such claims are properly documented and supported with valid claims of speciﬁc creditors, processed vouchers or payrolls, demand payments or bills; and
Maintain complete and updated subsidiary ledgers for Payable accounts. The payable accounts that have been outstanding for more than 2 years or more with no documents evidencing valid claims should be reverted to unappropriated surplus of the General Fund.

We recommended that Management adhere to Section 6.7 of COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994, by returning the unused balances of fund transfers to the source agencies amounting to ₱229,030.50. Ensure that trust funds are used exclusively to the purpose/s for which they were granted following the provisions of Section 4(3) of P.D. No. 1445 and Sections 305(e) and 309(b) of R.A. No. 7160; and
Require the settlement of long outstanding receivables to augment the deficient cash under trust fund.

We commended management for providing at least 5% of the total budget appropriations for GAD PPAs. Nevertheless, we recommended that management comply with the above-mentioned law and regulation by implementing PPAs which are gender-responsive and could address gender issues and concerns. Likewise, specific gender issue or concern should be identified for a particular GAD PPA and this must be stated in the GAD Plan and Budget and Accomplishment Report.
We recommended that Management release promptly the shares of the barangays in the municipal real property tax collections to augment their finances for programs, projects and activities.

We recommended that Management, thru the Bids and Awards Committee, ensure that all supporting contract documents are complete in compliance with pertinent provisions of R.A. No. 9184 and its IRR as amended by RA 12009 and its IRR, and submit within five (5) days from execution of contracts in accordance with Section 9.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001 and COA Circular No. 2012-001.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that Management:
Submit the Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRS) with complete supporting schedules for reconciling items;
Coordinate with the depositary bank to facilitate verification of unidentified bank credits and make the necessary adjusting entries; and
Make the necessary adjustments or replace the stale checks based on the prevailing guidelines set forth in PD 1445 and MNGAS for LGUs.
In view of the foregoing, we recommended that Management make the necessary reconciliation and adjustment of accounts to achieve fair presentation of the financial statements.

demand immediate and full liquidation of all cash advances of officers and employees that were already deemed accomplished or completed and if no action is taken by them, consider withholding their salaries, if warranted;
refrain from granting additional cash advances to officials and employees with previous unliquidated balances; and
adhere strictly to the provision of Section 89 of PD 1445 and follow the rules and regulations on the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances provided in COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.

require the employees concerned to submit a Certification by the Head of Agency as to the absolute necessity of the expenses. Otherwise, they should refund the excess amount. issue demand letters to the accountable officers with unliquidated cash advances. ensure subsidiary ledgers are maintained for all receivable transactions in compliance with Section 4(e) of the NGAS, to guarantee accurate account records and support efficient collection efforts.

create a system to store and manage supporting documents for transactions, reducing the risk of missing or incomplete records;
develop and enforce well-defined procedures for handling cash advances, ensuring timely liquidation and collection of receivables, and consider pursuing administrative and legal measures as mandated by COA Circulars to avoid the accumulation of long-overdue receivables and the deprivation of potential government income; and
initiate the write-off of dormant accounts as stipulated under COA Circular No. 2023-008, ensuring that all necessary documentation is prepared and procedural requirements are fully met.

prepares necessary adjusting entry to the affected accounts to ensure fair presentation of financial statements; and
causes the transfer of unexpended balance of DRRMF other than those appropriated for capital outlays to the Trust Fund, and record the same as Trust Liabilities – DRRMF.

prepare the necessary adjustments to reflect the accurate and correct balances of the inventories. initiate appropriate actions to obtain all pertinent documents related to the two (2) parcels of land and expedite their titling process to confirm ownership rights and protect the Municipality's property interests.

require the concerned Head of Offices to conduct a comprehensive analysis of all its liabilities and available cash and cash equivalents to ensure unhampered implementation of PPAs;
ensure that Trust Funds are used exclusively to the purpose/s for which they were granted following the provisions of Section 4(3) of P.D. No. 1445 and Sections 305(e) and 309(b) of R.A. No. 7160; and
require the settlement of long outstanding receivables to augment the deficient cash under Trust Fund.

We recommended that Management, thru the Bids and Awards Committee, ensure that all supporting contract documents are complete in compliance with pertinent provisions of R.A. No. 9184 and its IRR as amended by RA 12009 and its IRR, and submit within five (5) days from execution of contracts in accordance with Section 9.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001 and COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We recommended that the Management:
Require the Inventory Committee to conduct an annual and complete physical inventory of PPEs and immediately reconcile the results thereof with the accounting records to permit fair presentation of the account in the Statement of Financial Position; and
Consider disposing the unserviceable PPEs and submit request to COA for the derecognition of non-existing and missing PPEs from the Agency’s book of accounts, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020, otherwise known as the Guidelines and Procedures in the Conduct of Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE), Recognition of PPE Items Found at Station, and Disposition for Non-existing/Missing PPE Items, for the One-Time Cleansing of PPE Account Balances of Government Agencies.

We recommended that Management:
Perform a physical inventory of all plants and trees, reconcile the result with the accounting records and submit the report to the Office of the Auditor;
Restore the amounts derecognized and strictly follow the COA Circular No. 2020-006 guidelines for the proper derecognition/removal of LGU properties from the accounting records; and
In the event of property loss, promptly notify the Commission and submit an application for relief, along with supporting evidence, within 30 days or within a longer period if allowed, pursuant to Section 73 of P.D. No. 1445.

We recommended that the Management:
Submit a written explanation for not transferring the whole amount of the unexpended balance of the DRRMF to the STF; and
Transfer the unexpended balance of DRRMF at the end of each year, excluding amounts appropriated for capital outlays, to the Trust Fund and record it as Trust Liabilities – DRRMF.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Facilitate the tracing and locating of relevant documents to serve as basis in adjusting identified reconciling items in the respective reciprocal accounts;
Prepare a reconciliation report of reciprocal accounts and take up the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts to ensure that all reconciling items are resolved and/or adjusted on a timely basis; and
Perform constant review and appraisal of transactions to deter errors and omissions and include disclosure in the Notes to FS for fair presentation of Financial Statements.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to identify the variance, reconcile with BIR and prepare the necessary adjusting entries to reflect the accurate balance; and
Ensure the timely remittance of withheld taxes within the prescribed period to comply with regulatory requirements and avoid penalties.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to determine the root cause of the variance, reconcile historical balances with the respective agencies and prepare the necessary adjusting entries to maintain accurate liability reporting;
Coordinate with the respective agencies to resolve system-related issues preventing the remittance of premium contributions and loan amortizations for new hires and salary adjustments; and
Ensure timely remittance of premium contributions within the prescribed period to maintain compliance with regulatory requirements and avoid penalties.

We recommended that Management:
Expedite the implementation of all on-going programmed and funded projects under the 20% DF to optimize utilization thereof and to ensure timely delivery of intended benefits for the constituents of the Municipality;
Continuously ensure that the development projects that may be included under the 20% DF shall be those that are necessary, appropriate, or incidental to efficient and effective local governance, and those which are essential to the promotion of the general welfare of the people; and
Ensure that the development projects to be funded out of the 20% DF are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready.
We recommended that Management comply with the above-mentioned law and regulation by implementing PPAs which are gender-responsive and specifically address gender issues and concerns.

We recommended that Management:
Require the concerned Head of Offices to conduct a comprehensive analysis of all its liabilities and available cash and cash equivalents to ensure unhampered implementation of PPAs;
Ensure that trust funds are used exclusively to the purpose/s for which they were granted following the provisions of Section 4(3) of P.D. No. 1445 and Sections 305(e) and 309(b) of R.A. No. 7160; and
Require the settlement of long outstanding receivables to augment the deficient cash under trust fund.

We recommended that the Local School Board, thru its Secretariat, ensures that all relevant officials, including the Local Budget Officer, Local Accountant, and Local Treasurer, receive a copy of the SEF Budget within the stipulated 7-day period from its approval to avoid delay in the monitoring and control of the SEF.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the concerned personnel to submit the lacking documents and ensure they are properly tabbed/indexed for quick reference during evaluation; and
Designate responsible personnel to oversee, facilitate, and monitor the timely submission of all perfected contracts, purchase orders, and supporting documents in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-001.

We recommended that Management provide the necessary supporting documents of the transactions to avoid suspension in audit and strictly adhere to the provisions of Presidential Decree No. 1445, LBC 157 and COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We commended Management for the prompt remittance of the taxes withheld during the current year to the BIR. This notwithstanding, we recommend that Management continually ensure the withholding and timely remittance of taxes in conformity with Revenue Regulation No. 2-98 dated April 17, 1998.

We commended the Management for properly utilizing NGA funds in compliance with the above-cited regulations. Nevertheless, we recommend for management to continuously monitor and ensure that the transferred funds are utilized and implemented timely and used only for the purpose/s for which they were granted so that benefits intended to be derived therefrom will be achieved and delivered to beneficiaries.

We recommended that Management:
a.	Direct the Accounting and Supply Office to reconcile the PPE accounts with identified discrepancies to ensure accurate representation in the Statement of Financial Position; and
b.	Following reconciliation, evaluate unserviceable PPEs for disposal. Subsequently, submit a formal request to COA to derecognize PPEs that are missing or no longer exist, ensuring adherence to COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.

We recommended that Management:
a.	continuously require/demand the immediate settlement of the remaining overdue cash advances and if necessary, withhold the salaries of the concerned officials and employees for the settlement thereof; and
b.	Direct the Municipal Accountant to review the recorded Due from Officers and Employees and draw reclassification or adjusting entries, if necessary.

We recommended that Management through the Municipal Accountant to conduct verification, analysis, and validation of the existence of the said receivables and determine the concerned debtors to ascertain their collectibility. Likewise, develop and enforce well-defined procedures for collection of receivables to avoid the accumulation of long-overdue receivables and the deprivation of potential government income.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management:
conduct actual count or inventory of the breeding stocks and submit the corresponding result to the office of the audit team;
reconcile the accounting records against existing stocks and accordingly adjust the books of accounts; and
file for a Request for Relief from Property Accountability for the derecognition of Non-existing/Missing PPEs without Available Record of Accountability.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management through the Municipal Accountant to initiate the reversion of payables without valid claims to the Unappropriated Surplus of the General Fund in line with Section 98 of the PD No. 1445 for a more accurate and reliable Financial Statements of the Municipality.

We commended the Management for its consistent adherence to Section 287 of R.A. No. 7160 by providing the required amount out of its IRA for development projects. Nonetheless, we recommended that Management:
a. stop the practice of charging against 20% Development Fund for ineligible expenditures pursuant to the guidelines set forth under the DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1 dated November 4, 2020, to ensure optimal utilization of the funds;
b. direct the LFC to reevaluate the current programs/projects, more particularly on their corresponding expenditure items; and
c. review/evaluate carefully the expenditure items included in the proposed programs/projects budgeted under the 20% Development Fund as stated under Item 3.2.5 of the JMC No. 1 to ensure that what is planned and programmed is in compliance with the proper charging of the said fund.

We reiterated our recommendation that management, through the Municipal DRRM Officer, to utilize the LDRRMF strictly for implementation of projects and activities exclusively for disaster prevention, mitigation, preparedness, response, rehabilitation and recovery as envisioned in NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC JMC No. 2014-01, Section 5 of NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 and COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We commended management for providing at least 5% of the total budget appropriations for GAD PPAs. Nevertheless, we recommended that management comply with the above-mentioned law and regulation by implementing PPAs which are gender-responsive and could address gender issues and concerns. Likewise, specific gender issue or concern should be identified for a particular GAD PPA and this must be stated in the GAD Plan and Budget and Accomplishment Report.
We recommended that the Management release promptly the shares of the barangays in the municipal real property tax collections to augment their finances for programs, projects and activities.

We reiterated our recommendations that the management:
ensure that trust funds are used exclusively to the purpose/s for which they were granted following the provisions of Section 4(3) of P.D. No. 1445 and Sections 305(e) and 309(b) of R.A. No. 7160;
require the settlement of long outstanding receivables to augment the deficient cash under trust fund; and
ensure that trust obligations have sufficient cash back-up.

With the journal entry presented above, the Terminal Leave Benefits Payable
account of the agency was corrected accordingly. In addition, in line with the
observation on Leave Benefits Payable, we recommended and the Management
agreed to require the HR and Accounting Offices to establish a formalized process
where accumulated leave credits are accurately reported and timely recorded in
compliance with IPSAS 39. This includes periodic reconciliations, scheduled
reporting deadlines, and ensuring that the Accounting Office recognizes the full
leave benefits payable at the end of the reporting period.

To address the noted deficiencies, we recommended and the Management agreed
to the following actions:
a. To apply for the increase in the amount of the approved fidelity bond of the
Liquidating Officer to increase the maximum amount of accountability.
b. To avoid any personnel to take possession, control or custody of the government
funds unless with approved and appropriately bonded.
c. Strengthen internal controls by establishing regular monitoring systems to
track compliance with treasury circular requirements.
d. Ensure that the requisite documentation for fidelity bonding is prepared and
submitted in advance of any personnel changes or retirements.

To address the identified gaps, we recommended and the Management agreed to
provide the following instructions to the:
1. Accounting Personnel:
a. Reclassify the Current Portion of the Loan. The following journal entry is
recommended:
Debit: Non-Current Loans Payable-Domestic ₱1,550,000.00
Credit: Current Loans Payable-Domestic ₱1,550,000.00
b. Update the Notes to the Financial Statements to include detailed information
on the loan’s key terms such as the breakdown of principal and interest, the
quarterly amortization schedule, and collateral details as well as risk
management practices related to this liability.
2. Management establish a quality control mechanism by implementing a pre-
submission review by senior accountant to validate classifications, disclosures,
and IPSAS compliance. Utilize a standardized checklist aligned with IPSAS
and IPSAS 30 to ensure all key loan disclosure items are accurately reported.
Additionally, mandate IPSAS training for accounting staff to enhance their
understanding of liability classification and financial instrument disclosures,
reinforcing accuracy and compliance.

To address the noted observation, we recommended and the Management agreed
to:
a. Effect the necessary adjustments in the records and reports maintained by
both the Accounting and Treasury Offices and promptly notify the audit team
once these corrections have been completed.
b. Develop and implement a formal, written policy outlining the timely
reconciliation process between the Accounting and Treasury Offices. This
policy should clearly define the frequency of reconciliations, assign specific
responsibilities, and establish procedures for addressing discrepancies.
c. Strengthen internal controls by enhancing oversight within the cash
management process. This will help prevent errors and reduce the risk of
financial misstatements.
d. Improve interdepartmental communication by establishing regular
coordination meetings or progress reports between the Accounting and
Treasury Departments to ensure prompt identification and resolution of
discrepancies.
e. Provide regular staff training involved in cash management and reconciliation
activities to ensure they are well-versed in the established procedures and best
practices.

To address the identified deficiencies and mitigate the associated risks, we
recommended and the Management agreed to implement the following actions:
a. Develop and Enforce Comprehensive Policies and Procedures:
Draft a detailed Financial Assistance Manual inspired by DSWD’s AICS
program that outlines clear eligibility criteria, tiered assistance rates based on
need and crisis severity, and required documentation (e.g., medical
certificates, death certificates, barangay validation). This manual should also
include standardized assessment tools to objectively evaluate beneficiary
needs, and staff should receive regular training to ensure consistent
implementation. Clear, structured guidelines will eliminate arbitrary decision-
making, ensure consistency, and comply with COA’s mandate for documented
controls.
b. Create a Centralized Beneficiary Database:
Implement a digital tracking system to cross-check beneficiaries against
previous claims and verify identities using national IDs or barangay records.
A centralized database minimizes the risk of duplicate claims and fraud.
c. Strengthen Transparency and Accountability Mechanisms:
Publicize eligibility criteria and disbursement details on MGPQ’s website or
public bulletin boards, establish a Public Assistance Desk for grievances and
appeals, and issue quarterly reports on fund utilization and program impact to
enhance transparency, build public trust, and align with ethical governance
principles.
d. Train Personnel on Policy Adherence:
Conduct regular workshops for LGU staff on applying structured guidelines
consistently, identifying and escalating ethical concerns, and collaborating
with barangay social workers for beneficiary validation to ensure uniform
implementation.
e. Enhance Documentation and Validation:
Require comprehensive case summaries and social assessments by qualified
personnel, such as social workers, to validate beneficiary needs and justify aid
amounts with objective data to create a reliable audit trail and meet best
practice standards for validated support.
f. Implement Post-Disbursement Monitoring:
Conduct random audits of beneficiaries to verify that funds are used for their
intended purposes and to ensure that duplicate claims are not processed.
Ongoing monitoring reinforces accountability and deters misuse of public
funds.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Engineer to provide a copy of SWA on infrastructure projects to the Municipal Budget Officer which will serve as her basis in the preparation of the CAFOA, furnish the same to the Municipal Accountant to facilitate the preparation of the necessary journal entry vouchers to record the cost of implemented projects taking into account their status of implementation, thus, the corresponding PPE and Accounts Payable accounts will be fairly presented in the financial statements.

observations.recommendation:

Deposit the collections intact daily or as soon as it reaches the threshold as prescribed by Section 32, Volume I of the MNGAS for LGUs and Section 28 of COA Circular No. 92-382 dated July 3, 1992 in order to avoid possible loss or misuse of the undeposited collection and enables the Municipality to earn interest that could accrue from these collections.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE oblige either the BAC or the Municipal Engineer to monitor the submission of the CHSP duly approved by the DOLE from the winning bidders before the signing of contract to ensure the protection and welfare of workers and the general public within and around the immediate vicinity of the construction worksite. observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Management to:

Stop using the PCF in the payment of regular expenses such as but not limited to financial, medical and educational assistance; and

Comply strictly to Section 48 of Volume I, Manual on NGAS for LGUs and COA Circular 97-002 to avoid irregularities on government transactions.
observations.managementAction: The Management stopped in using PCF for payment of regular expenses.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended to Management the following:

The Municipal Treasurer, to not allow delayed or partial remittances of collections from RCCs;

The Municipal Accountant, to see to it that all collections and remittances are recorded on appropriate time or year; and

All Accountable Officers (AOs) concerned, to exercise due diligence in the collection, remittance and safekeeping otherwise, impose sanctions against erring AOs.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Treasurer has mandated her collectors to remit regularly, weekly remittance every Monday for collectors assigned to far flung areas, or when their collections reached the amount of ₱5,000.00.

Reconciliation between the Treasury Office and Accounting Office are being done monthly to ensure that all collections and remittances were recorded.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Management to instruct the Municipal Treasurer to:

Develop an effective tax collection campaign program to enhance the collection of RPT and SET receivables;

Prepare a certified list of all real property tax delinquencies and post the said list at the Municipality and in publicly accessible and conspicuous place in each Barangay and published in a newspaper of general circulation in the Municipality once a week for two (2) consecutive weeks; and

Issue notice of delinquency and or final demand letter to concerned property owner and avail remedies thru levy on real property or judicial actions provided under RA 7160.
observations.managementAction:
House to house tax collection campaign have been implemented, as a result, the collection rate of the Municipality increased in CY 2024

The Municipal Treasurer prepared the certified list of RPT delinquencies and posted the same to each Barangay Office.

The Municipal Treasurer issued notice of delinquency to the concerned property owners.

observations.recommendation:
We commended the Management for ensuring the allocation of LDRRMF to support disaster risk management activities and complying with pertinent laws, rules and regulations in the recording, utilization and reporting of LDRRMF. However, we recommended that Management:

Thru the LDRRMC, continuously monitor, coordinate and evaluate PPAs to ensure the completion of the most urgent, needed, appropriate and eligible projects; and

Ensure efficient and effective implementation of all programmed projects identified during the budget year to optimize the utilization of the fund and to assure that the goals and objectives of reducing disaster risk and enhancing disaster preparedness and response capabilities be achieved.
observations.managementAction: LDRRM Officer monitors and ensures that all activities charged against the fund are in compliance with the regulation.
observations.reason: Though procurement activities were being performed early, there were uncontrolled events that would delay the implementation of the PPAs like failed biddings, subsequent disqualification of Lowest Calculated Bidder, weather conditions, etc.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Management, through the Municipal DRRM Officer, to utilize the LDRRMF strictly for implementation of projects and activities exclusively for disaster prevention, mitigation, preparedness, response, rehabilitation and recovery as envisioned in NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC JMC No. 2014-01, Section 5 of NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 and COA Circular No. 2012-002.
observations.managementAction: LDRRM Officer monitors and ensures that all activities charged against the fund are in compliance with the regulation.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

Require the concerned Head of Offices to conduct a comprehensive analysis of all its liabilities and available cash and cash equivalents to ensure unhampered implementation of PPAs;

Ensure that Trust Funds are used exclusively to the purpose/s for which they were granted following the provisions of Section 4(3) of P.D. No. 1445 and Sections 305(e) and 309(b) of R.A. No. 7160; and

Require the settlement of long outstanding receivables to augment the deficient cash under Trust Fund.
observations.managementAction:
A review and analysis of the accounts were made and as a result, some errors in recording were already adjusted.

The Management monitors and makes sure that Trust Funds are being used exclusively to the purpose/s for which they were granted.
observations.reason: Most of the Accountable Officers are already resigned or deceased which made the collection difficult.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the BAC/BAC Secretariat to strictly observe Section 37.4 of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of R.A. No. 9184 and ensure that the Notice to Proceed are issued and received by the successful bidders within seven (7) calendar days from the date of contract approval.

We recommended the Local Chief Executive to require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that taxes are correctly computed and deducted from compensation income of municipal officials and employees in accordance with the Revised Withholding Tax Table as indicated in Revenue Regulation No. 11-2018.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following to the Management:

The Municipal Engineer shall provide the Municipal Accountant with the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects;

The LGU shall maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map and shall keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components; and

The Municipal Accountant shall prepare the Journal Voucher to record the inventory of the local road network and its components in the general ledger and the Local Road Network Ledger Card.  He shall keep and maintain subsidiary records for roads and road components for every road network, and ensure that 100% of the value of road network of the LGU shall be booked-up in 2019 consistent with COA Circular No. 2015-008. Full disclosure on road networks should be made in the Notes to the Financial Statements of the LGU.

Make the necessary adjustments so as to arrive at the correct balances of the affected accounts in the financial statements.

S. 2017 and JC No. 1 S. of 2020.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipality:
Fully implement/utilize the planned programs, activities and projects under the DRRM Fund so that the intended benefits would be fully achieved; and

Revisit the feasibility/responsiveness of its LDRRMIP in order to prevent delay in the implementation of its planned programs/ projects/activities.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management strictly monitor and fast track the implementation and completion of all the LGU’s programs, projects and activities funded under the 20% Development Fund so that the intended benefits would be fully achieved.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant disclose in the Notes to FS relevant information on the breakdown/details of the accounts, the significant changes in the balances or the unchanged balances, and the reasons thereof. Ensure that other necessary disclosures are provided in the Notes to FS in order to enhance the understandability and reliability of the financial statements in conformity with the pertinent IPSASs.
