observations.managementAction: Management was not able to comply with the regulations on appropriate charges against the 20% Development Fund.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management should strictly comply with COA Circular No. 77-61 which provides the Manual on Audit for Fuel Consumption of Government Motor Vehicles. Any unnecessary and excessive fuel consumption shall be disallowed in audit, if warranted.
observations.managementAction: Management did not submit the required report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Engineer to provide a copy of SWA on infrastructure projects to the Municipal Budget Officer which will serve as her basis in the preparation of the CAFOA, furnish the same to the Municipal Accountant to facilitate the preparation of the necessary journal entry vouchers to record the cost of implemented projects taking into account their status of implementation, thus, the corresponding PPE and Accounts Payable accounts will be fairly presented in the financial statements.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the Inventory Committee to follow the guidelines and procedures as provided for in COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 in the conduct of physical count of PPE for the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts of the Municipality to establish the existence, condition and accuracy of PPE account balances. Moreover, require them to derecognize all tangible items below the capitalization threshold and follow the prescribed procedures in the disposition of unserviceable properties in order not to overstate the PPE and related accounts and to ascertain the fair presentation of the same in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to exert extra effort to locate records of these receivables and payables. If such efforts exerted are futile, apply then the procedures prescribed under Item 7.0 of COA Circular 2023-008, dated August 17, 2023. Likewise, revert the undocumented payables to the unappropriated surplus of the general fund in conformity with Section 98 of PD 1445.

We recommended that the LCE require the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to maintain Real Property Tax Account Register/Taxpayer’s Index Card to identify the list of taxpayers and uncollected amounts that would support the established receivables from real property taxes and to monitor tax collections and delinquencies. Also, instruct him to furnish the Municipal Accountant a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year as the basis of the latter in recording RPT and SET Receivables at the beginning of every year.

We recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Accountant to course thru all regular purchases of supplies in the applicable inventory accounts and issuances thereof shall only be recorded as expense as they take place. Also, submit the SSMI, original copy of RIS, Certified List of Recipients and RPCI to support the recorded issuances of inventories and ending balance of inventories to avoid suspension in audit. Moreover, instruct the Supply Officer to consolidate the weekly RIS of supplies and materials using the SSMI and submit the same to the Municipal Accountant as his basis in recording the issuances of inventories to the end-users to establish the reliability of Inventory and related expense accounts.

We recommended that the LCE require the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to:
Closely supervise and monitor the collectors and ensure that the latter promptly remit their collection on a daily basis as required by Section 29, Volume 1 of the MNGAS for LGUs.
Deposit the collections intact daily or as soon as it reaches the threshold as prescribed by Section 32, Volume I of the MNGAS for LGUs and Section 28 of COA Circular No. 92-382 dated July 3, 1992 in order to avoid possible loss or misuse of the undeposited collection and enables the Municipality to earn interest that could accrue from these collections.

We recommended that the LCE through the BAC to strictly adhere to the pertinent provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of the RA 9184 on the posting requirements of bid opportunities and notices to ensure transparency and accountability in the procurement process.

We recommended that the LCE require the BAC to evaluate the bids/quotations of cooperatives strictly in accordance with the provisions of Appendix 25 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 to ensure a fair and competitive comparison of bids/quotations in all procurements of the Municipality.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the BAC to strictly adhere to the pertinent provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184, resort to Negotiated Procurement only after Two Failed Biddings. Moreover, require the submission of the necessary complete documentation to the Audit Team to establish the legality, validity, and propriety of the transactions.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the BAC to revisit the pertinent provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184 and recommend the appropriate mode of procurement, thus the legality, validity and propriety of the transactions could be ascertained.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the BAC to include all procurement activities in the APP. Moreover, require the end-users or implementing units to prepare all documents necessary for the procurement activity, including but not be limited to, the technical specifications, scope of work and plans/drawings.

We recommended that the LCE oblige either the BAC or the Municipal Engineer to monitor the submission of the CHSP duly approved by the DOLE from the winning bidders before the signing of contract to ensure the protection and welfare of workers and the general public within and around the immediate vicinity of the construction worksite. Also, require the Municipal Accountant to see to it that payments of infrastructure projects are supported with valid Certificate of CHSP approved by the DOLE, otherwise, deduct the corresponding cost of implementing the same from the contractor’s claim to avoid disallowance in audit. Moreover, require the Municipal Engineer to ensure that appropriate safety measures are employed by the contractor so as to ensure protection and welfare of workers and the general public from any untoward incident that may occur during the implementation of the project.

We recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Accountant to cause the immediate submission of the lacking documents and justifications on the deficiencies observed for further evaluation of the transactions and to avoid suspension in audit. Also, oblige the Municipal Accountant to see to it that disbursement vouchers are duly supported with the required basic documents to ensure their validity and propriety.

We recommended that the LCE oblige the Municipal Accountant to cause the immediate submission of the DVs duly supported with complete documents for audit and verification purposes. Moreover, Management strictly observes the timely submission of transaction documents to facilitate the review thereon and avoid further audit action as provided under Section 122 (2) of PD No. 1445.  Thus, any deficiencies observed in the verification and evaluation of the transactions could be immediately communicated to management for appropriate corrective action thereof.

We recommended that the LCE require the Implementing Unit the prompt implementation of all programs/projects funded out of the tobacco excise tax to give the constituents, especially the tobacco farmers, the early benefits that could be derived therefrom.

We recommended that the LCE enforce the immediate settlement of the audit suspensions as prescribed in the RRSA under COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009. Moreover, require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that supporting documents of transactions are complete, proper and valid in order to prevent the same from being suspended/ disallowed in post-audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant continue her effort in complying the timely preparation of Bank Reconciliation Statements for fair presentation of Cash in Bank account of the Municipality.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant recognize impairment allowance on dormant and uncollectible accounts.

We recommended that management act assertively on the disallowance issued and enforce the immediate refund of such disallowance considering that loss of government funds was already incurred. Tolerating delayed refunds would further contribute to additional loss of government funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant maintain the Report of the Physical Count of Biological Assets (RPCBA) as provided under the Manual and the inventory of breeding stocks and the prevailing market price for each item of biological asset as of reporting date and the Municipal Agriculture Officer to provide the inventory of breeding stocks and other biological assets, if any.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile the Net Cash Flows from Operating Activities to Surplus and disclose the same on the face of the Cash Flow Statement or in the Notes to Financial Statements in compliance with Section 29 of IPSAS No. 2.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct all the Department Heads concerned to submit their detailed action plans on the CY 2022 AAR findings and recommendations. The information gathered therein be used to properly accomplish the Agency Action Plan and Status of Implementation and submit the same to the Audit Team for evaluation of the actions taken and status of implementation by the Municipality on the audit recommendations in compliance with Section 97 of the General Provisions of RA 11639. Likewise, ensure thereafter that a status report on the actions taken on the audit findings and recommendations be submitted to COA within sixty (60) days from the date of receipt of AAR.

We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive to designate a focal person to consolidate the Agency Action Plan and Status of Implementation (AAPSI) from different Offices to facilitate the submission of report to the Audit Team.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer deposit all collections as required by the rules and regulations to prevent misappropriation, theft and loss of funds. Likewise, proper handling of collections and its deposits be strictly observed and deposits should be attributed/traced from the collected amounts. Impose sanctions to the erring accountable officers, if warranted.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer prepare and submit at the beginning of the year to the Audit Team the List of all on-going government projects, programs/activities (PPAs) and those that are to be implemented during the year in compliance with COA Circular No. 2013-004.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Bids and Awards Committee to prepare and submit to the GPPB the Procurement Monitoring Report (PMR) within the time frame stated in Section 12.2 of the IRR of R.A. No. 9184 as reiterated in GPPB Resolution No. 11-2020, dated May 20, 2020 for monitoring and compliance with the provisions of the Procurement Law.

We, therefore recommended that Management always ensure the ear tagging of Breeding Stocks for inventory control purposes and to ensure the proper handling and management thereof. It is likewise recommended the installation of sound internal control in order to prevent loss thru proper identification of Breeding Stocks.

We recommended that the:
Local Chief Executive ensure the optimum utilization of the 20% DF by promptly implementing the priority development projects as reflected in its duly approved local development plans and establish the effective and efficient implementation of the projects to maximize the developmental benefits for the intended beneficiaries;
MPDC (i) initiate close coordination with the Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Accountant for the proper monitoring of project implementation and the provision of all the necessary information in the Status Report of Government PPAs before submission to the Audit Team; and
Municipal Engineer (i) require timely implementation of PPAs in coordination with the MPDC under their responsibility at all times.

We recommended Management to:
direct the Municipal Engineer that repair should be undertaken by applying concrete epoxy to avoid deterioration on the Road Concreting of Tobacco Producing Barangay located at Manaoag, Quirino, Isabela;
direct the Contractor to deliver the unaccomplished shoulders of the Road Concreting of Tobacco Producing Barangay located at Sto. Domingo, Quirino, Isabela, otherwise the amount of ₱55,745.10 representing the unaccomplished shouldering shall be deducted from the contract cost or shall be refunded by the contractor, if already paid; and
request immediately for re-inspection from the Audit Team after undertaking the necessary remedies on the observations noted above for validation purposes.

We recommended that management fast track the prompt implementation of all projects/programs funded out of the tobacco excise taxes in order to give the constituents early benefits that could be derived therefrom.

We recommended that:
The Municipal Treasurer (MT) furnish the Municipal Accountant (MA) a duly certified list showing the name of the tax payers and the amount due;
The MA to prepare adjusting entries to record prior years’ delinquent receivable and set-up at the beginning of the year the current year’s receivable for RPT and SET equivalent to 1 percent of the total assessed value of the real property; and
Strict compliance to Section 246 of R.A. No. 7160 or the Local Government Code and Section 20 of the Manual on the NGAS, Volume 1 for LGUs for fair presentation of receivables and deferred income accounts in the financial statements of the Municipality.
The MT stated during the exit conference that with the help of the MASSO, they have already updated the list of delinquent tax payers and their amount due. The same report was also forwarded to the MACCO for appropriate action.
The MA stated that his office is going to prepare the necessary adjusting entries to reflect the correct balance of the said accounts. He committed to religiously set-up at the beginning of the year the current year receivables.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Analyze the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds accounts, trace back differences and the nature of the accounts;
Prepare the necessary adjusting entries to bring the correct balances of the accounts; and
Maintain subsidiary ledger as necessary and include disclosure in the Notes to FS for fair presentation of Financial Statements.
The MA commented during the exit that his team has already started tracing back and was able to decrease the variance noted. They also found out that the difference is due to erroneous recording using “Subsidy To/From Other Funds” for years. Since the difference represents accumulated amounts including prior years’ error, his team could not resolve it immediately, but gradually.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management:
Conduct actual annual inventory count of all supplies on stock by the Municipality as at year-end; and
Prioritize the maintenance of stock cards and supplies ledger cards and the reconciliation of balances with the accounting records. Prepare adjusting entries, if any, to reflect the correct balance of the affected accounts.
The Management admitted the observation and committed to comply with the recommendations.
The Municipal Mayor commented that the Department Heads will lead the physical count of inventories located in their own offices and he will assign the newly designated General Services Officer (GSO) to oversee the overall physical count of inventories and consolidation of reports.

We recommended the Management to require the Inventory Committee to conduct an annual and complete physical inventory of PPEs and immediately reconcile the results thereof with the accounting records to permit fair presentation of the Property, Plant and Equipment account in the Statement of Financial Position. Likewise, the Accounting Office and General Services Office to prepare and continuously maintain PPE ledger cards and property cards, respectively.
The Management admitted the observation and assured that they will comply with the recommendation.
The Municipal Mayor also instructed the Department Heads to lead the physical count of properties and equipment in their offices.

We recommended that Management:
Continuously require/demand the immediate settlement of the remaining overdue cash advances and if necessary, withhold the salaries of the concerned officials and employees for the settlement thereof; and
Strictly comply with the provisions of Section 89 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 97 – 002 dated February 10, 1997 in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.
The MA commented that they will send demand letters to the Accountable Officers and will request write-off of accounts of those deceased Accountable Officers (AOs). He also added that he will withhold portion of the salaries of the erring AOs until full payment or liquidation, if the Municipal Mayor permits.

We recommended to Management the following:
The MT, to not allow delayed or partial remittances of collections from RCCs;
The MA, to see to it that all collections and remittances are recorded on appropriate time or year; and
All collectors concerned, to exercise due diligence in the collection, remittance and safekeeping otherwise, impose sanctions against erring collectors.
The MT stated that her office is now stricter in the issuance of Accountable Forms (AFs) to the collectors. From the time they received the Audit Observation Memorandum on collections and remittances, the MT requires remittance first before issuance of new or additional AFs. She also demanded the collectors to remit their unremitted collections.
The Management commented that they will continue to ensure that the transferred funds are utilized and implemented timely and will be used only for the purpose/s for which they were granted.

We recommended that Management submit the required documents to support and justify the payments made to avoid suspension in audit. Ensure that all disbursements are in accordance with rules and regulations, otherwise, payments made contrary thereof shall be disallowed in audit, if warranted.
The Management expressed apology for the inadequacy and assured that they will submit the missing supporting documents to justify the payments.

We recommended that Management:
Expedite the implementation of all on-going programmed and funded projects under the 20% DF to optimize utilization thereof and to ensure timely delivery of intended benefits for the constituents of the Municipality;
Continuously ensure that the development projects that may be included under the 20% DF shall be those that are necessary, appropriate, or incidental to efficient and effective local governance, and those which are essential to the promotion of the general welfare of the people; and
Ensure that the development projects to be funded out of the 20% DF are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready.
The Management agreed with the recommendation and assured compliance.

We commended the Management for ensuring the allocation of LDRRMF to support disaster risk management activities and complying with pertinent laws, rules and regulations in the recording, utilization and reporting of LDRRMF. However, we recommended that Management:
Thru the LDRRMC, continuously monitor, coordinate and evaluate PPAs to ensure the completion of the most urgent, needed, appropriate and eligible projects; and
Ensure efficient and effective implementation of all programmed projects identified during the budget year to optimize the utilization of the fund and to assure that the goals and objectives of reducing disaster risk and enhancing disaster preparedness and response capabilities be achieved.
The Management commented that they will continuously monitor, coordinate and evaluate PPAs to ensure efficient and effective implementation of all programmed projects.

We recommended the Management to prepare and submit the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF as required under COA Circular No. 2012-002 to ensure proper monitoring, control and documentation of Disaster Funds.
The Management admitted the observation and committed to comply with the recommendation.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that withholding of taxes are correctly computed and deducted from compensation income of municipal officials and employees in accordance with the Revised Withholding Tax Table as indicated in Revenue Regulation No. 11-2018.
The MA commented that from the time they received the Audit Observation Memorandum, his office started the withholding of tax.

We recommended that Management strictly comply with the provisions of BIR Revenue Memorandum Order No. 23-2014 on the withholding of taxes to help the government in generating revenues to meet its appropriation requirement and to avoid penalties and legal action against the erring withholding agents from the BIR.
The MA stated during the exit conference that they started withholding of 5 percent VAT on payments to QUIRELCO when they received the AOM.

We recommended the MA in coordination with the MBO to prepare and come up with the Statement of Comparison of Budget and Actual Amounts (SCBAA) as required under regulations to optimize the use of the complete set of financial statements.
The MA admitted the liability in his part and promised to prepare and submit the said report in 2024.
The MT and MA agreed with the recommendation and stated that they will identify the liable persons as soon as possible and will issue demand letters.

We recommended the Management to provide the necessary supporting documents of the transactions to avoid suspension in audit and strictly adhere to the provisions of Presidential Decree No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001.
The Management admitted the observation and agreed to submit the necessary supporting documents.

We recommended that Management, through the GSO to require each office to prepare and submit their Monthly Report of Fuel Consumptions for consolidation and for the driver of each of the motor vehicle of the Municipality to prepare at the end of the month. A Monthly Report of Official Travels, summarizing in chronological order his trips for the month which shall be certified by the concerned driver and approved by the Municipal Mayor or his/her authorized representative to ensure that fuel consumption is effectively monitored and used only for official businesses in conformity with COA Circular 77-61.
The Management stated during the exit conference that they will gradually comply with the recommendation since the drivers of the Municipality are still learning and in the process of familiarizing the forms.

We recommended that Management stop the practice of allowing reimbursements to officials and employees and enforce direct payment to suppliers or creditors as prescribed under Section 93 of PD 1445 and RA9184.
The Management agreed with the observation and promised to comply with the recommendations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, thru the Bids and Awards Committee, to strictly monitor compliance by the BAC Secretariat of its functions under R.A. No. 9184 and its IRR to ensure that the necessary procurement activities are undertaken and that documentary requirements are properly accomplished, and submitted to the concerned offices, put index tabs/tabbings on the documents to be submitted to facilitate checking thereof.
The Management assured compliance to the recommendations.

We recommended that the Management continue the practice of depositing collections regularly to comply with the rules and regulations as mandated by Section 69 of P.D. No. 1445 and Section 32 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I on collections and deposits.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
analyze the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds accounts, trace back differences and the nature of the accounts;
prepare the necessary adjusting entries to bring the correct balances of the accounts; and
maintain subsidiary ledger as necessary and include disclosure in the Notes to FS for fair presentation of Financial Statements.

We commended management for its effort in enforcing the liquidation of substantial amount of cash advances. Nevertheless, we recommended the Management to:
continuously require/demand the immediate settlement of the remaining overdue cash advances and if necessary, withhold the salaries of the concerned officials and employees for the settlement thereof; and
strictly comply with the provisions of Section 89 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 97 – 002 dated February 10, 1997 in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.

We recommended to Management the following:
Direct the Inventory Committee to conduct the semestral, physical count of inventory items by type, and prepare the Report of Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI). Thereafter, submit a copy to the Auditor, pursuant to Section 124 of the MNGAS for LGUs;
Instruct the General Services Office to consolidate weekly the Requisition Issue Slip using the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued. Based on the SSMI, a JEV shall be prepared by the Municipal Accountant pursuant to Section 121 of the MNGAS for LGUs; and
Prepare the necessary adjustment to reflect the actual correct balances thereof. Likewise, both records of the Municipal Accountant’s Office and General Services Office should be adjusted accordingly in order to present fairly the financial position of the Municipality.

We recommended Management to require the Inventory Committee to conduct an annual and complete physical inventory of PPEs and immediately reconcile the results thereof with the accounting records to permit fair presentation of the Property, Plant and Equipment account in the Statement of Financial Position. Likewise, the Accounting Office and General Services Office should prepare and maintain PPE ledger cards and property cards, respectively.

We recommended the Management to instruct all concerned to:
conduct actual count or inventory of the biological assets and submit the corresponding result to the office of the audit team;
reconcile the accounting records against existing stocks especially those with known beneficiaries and accordingly adjust the books of accounts; and
revisit the agreement of the municipality in its animal dispersal scheme and various proponents as provided in their Memoranda of Agreement and faithfully adhere to the conditions set therein.

We recommended to Management thru the Municipal Accountant to:
trace the original entry made and make the necessary adjustments on the outstanding payable accounts and ensure creditor’s claims are properly supported with valid documents, processed vouchers or payrolls, and demand payments or bills; and
maintain a complete and updated subsidiary ledgers for Payable accounts. The payable accounts that has been outstanding for more than 2 years or more with no documents evidencing valid claims should be reverted to Unappropriated Surplus of the General Fund.

We recommended that the Management instruct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to require the conduct of reconciliation of cashbook and accounting records at least quarterly or as the need arises to ensure timely discovery of errors and to facilitate early preparation of applicable adjustments.

We recommended to Management the following:
the Municipal Treasurer, to not allow delayed or partial remittances of collections from RCCs;
the Municipal Accountant, to see to it that all collections and remittances are recorded on appropriate time or year; and
all Accountable Officers (AOs) concerned, to exercise due diligence in the collection, remittance and safekeeping otherwise, impose sanctions against erring AOs.

We commended the Management for the utilization of the funds received from the NGAs in compliance with the above-cited regulations. Nevertheless, we recommended for Management to continuously monitor and ensure that the transferred funds are utilized and implemented timely and used only for the purpose/s for which they were granted so that benefits intended to derived therefrom will be achieved and delivered to beneficiaries.

We recommended that Management:
expedite the implementation of all on – going programmed and funded projects under the 20% MDF to optimize utilization thereof and to ensure timely delivery of intended benefits for the constituents of the Municipality;
continuously ensure that the development projects that may be included under the 20% DF are necessary, appropriate, or incidental in efficient and effective local governance, and those which are essential to the promotion of the general welfare of the people; and
ensure that the development projects to be funded out of the 20% DF are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready.

We recommended the Management, through the Municipal DRRM Officer, to utilize the LDRRMF strictly for implementation of projects and activities exclusively for disaster prevention, mitigation, preparedness, response, rehabilitation and recovery as envisioned in NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC JMC No. 2014-01, Section 5 of NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 and COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We reiterated our recommendation for Management to prepare and submit the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF as required under COA Circular No. 2012-002 to ensure proper monitoring, control and documentation of Disaster Funds.

We commended the Management for ensuring the allocation of LDRRMF to support disaster risk management activities and complying with pertinent laws, rules and regulations in the recording, utilization and reporting of LDRRMF. However, we also recommended that Management:
thru the LDRRMC, continuously monitor, coordinate and evaluate PPAs to ensure the completion of the most urgent, needed, appropriate and eligible projects; and
ensure efficient and effective implementation of all programmed projects identified during the budget year to optimize the utilization of the fund and to assure that the goals and objectives of reducing disaster risk and enhancing disaster preparedness and response capabilities be achieved.

We recommended the Management:
through the Municipal Accountant to conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis, and validation of the existence of the receivables and determine the concerned debtors to ascertain their collectability. If proven to be no longer collectible, request for authority to write-off dormant receivable accounts and attach required documents prescribed under COA Circular No. 2016-005; and
the Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary ledgers in accordance with Section 4 (e) of the NGAS to ensure complete information on the accounts and to facilitate the collection thereof.

We recommended that Management:
Stop the practice of charging expenses/claims without an approved appropriation; and
Implement the currents year’s PAPs as programmed.

We recommended the:
Local Chief Executive, thru the Bids and Awards Committee, to strictly monitor compliance by the BAC Secretariat of its functions under R.A. No. 9184 and its IRR to ensure that the necessary procurement activities are undertaken and that documentary requirements are properly accomplished and submitted to the concerned offices; and
Municipal Accountant to ensure the completeness of supporting documents before payment to contractors/suppliers as her function under Section 474(b)(5) of R.A. No. 7160.
We recommended that the responsible personnel to accomplish the documentary requirements and put index tabs/tabbings on the documents to be submitted to facilitate checking thereof to ensure that all lacking documents are submitted.

We recommended that Management strictly comply with the provisions of BIR Revenue Memorandum Order No. 23-2014 on the withholding of taxes to help the government in generating revenues to meet its appropriation requirement and to avoid penalties and legal action against the erring withholding agents from the BIR.

We recommended the Local Chief Executive to require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that withholding of taxes are correctly computed and deducted from compensation income of municipal officials and employees in accordance with the Revised Withholding Tax Table as indicated in Revenue Regulation No. 11-2018.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to revisit and review the withheld taxes on the transactions of the Municipality and make the necessary adjustments in the books of the Municipality.
If additional remittance and amendment to the BIR is necessary, we recommended the Management require the designated officials who are primarily responsible to settle the penalties and charges thereto.
Moreover, we recommended the Management to ensure compliance to BIR regulations to avoid the imposition of penalties and disallowance in audit.

We recommended that within a period not exceeding ten (10) calendar days from the receipt of Notice of Award by the bidder with the LCRB, HRRB, SCRB, or SRRB, the HoPE or his duly authorized representative shall enter into a contract agreement with the winning bidder to ensure the early implementation of projects.

We recommended the BAC/BAC Secretariat to strictly observe Section 37.4 of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of R.A. No. 9184 and ensure that the Notice to Proceed are issued and received by the successful bidders within seven (7) calendar days from the date of contract approval.

We recommended that Management, through the Municipal Accountant to recompute the claims for hazard pay of the Social Worker and include only the actual number of days of exposure to occupational risk as provided in Section 16 of the IRR of R.A. No. 9433, and thereafter, cause the refund of the excess payments erroneously made.

We recommend that Management, through the GSO to require each offices to prepare and submit their Monthly Report of Fuel Consumptions for consolidation and  for the driver of each of the motor vehicle of the LGU to prepare at the end of the month, a Monthly Report of Official Travels, summarizing in chronological order his trips for the month which shall be certified by him and approved by the LCE or his/her authorized representative to ensure that fuel consumption is effectively monitored and used only for official businesses in conformity with COA Circular 77-61.

We commended the Management for the optimal utilization of the fund in support to needs for the operation and maintenance of public schools. We also recommended the Management through the Local School Board, being the one responsible in the formulation, approval, and utilization of the SEF Budget as provided under RA No. 7160 or the Local Government Code to:
continuously sustain its efforts in ensuring that all budgeted projects, programs and activities are optimally and fully implemented;
prioritize and allocate more funds for the construction, repair and maintenance of school buildings and other facilities of public schools; and
revisit the revised guidelines on the use of SEF under DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017 to ensure a more strategic and efficient formulation and utilization of resources for priority programs and projects.

We recommended the Management to formulate a well-defined policy and guidelines on donation/financial assistance to provide equal opportunity for assistance to the constituents of the Municipality containing but not limited to the following:
Kind of financial assistance (medical, educational, burial, etc.);
Approved amount per specific case, including the frequency of grants to each individual; and
Criteria in granting the donation and the documentary requirements for each particular case.

We recommended that Management stop the practice of allowing reimbursements to officials and employees and enforce direct payment to suppliers or creditors as prescribed under Section 93 of P.D. No. 1445 and R.A. No. 9184.
We recommended the Management to provide justification on the paid globe plan excess usage. These expenses are subject for disallowance if proven unnecessary.

We recommended that Management instruct:
The Municipal Treasurer, to not allow delayed or partial remittances of collections from RCCs;
The Municipal Accountant, to see to it that all collections and remittances are recorded on appropriate time or year; and
All Accountable Officers (AOs) concerned, to exercise due diligence in the collection, remittance and safekeeping otherwise, impose sanctions against erring AOs.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries for bank reconciling items which understates various cash accounts, exert efforts to identify those undetermined reconciling items/differences and ensure that the bank reconciliation statements are duly supported upon submission to the Office of the Auditor. Henceforth, strictly adhere to the provisions of Par. 27 of International Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS) 1 and Items 3.1 to 3.4 of COA Circular No. 96-011.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile the reciprocal accounts and adjust the balances in the financial statements. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant review the reciprocal accounts on a regular basis to ensure the accuracy and reliability of the accounts.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that Management:
Demand immediate and full liquidation of all cash advances by officials and employees that were already deemed accomplished or completed to this day and if no action is taken by them, consider to withhold their salaries, if warranted;
Refrain from granting additional cash advances to officials and employees with previous unliquidated balances; and
Strictly adhere to Sec. 89 of PD 1445 and follow the rules and regulations on the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances provided in COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.

We recommended that Management require:
The Inventory Committee to conduct actual count of the remaining balance of purchased Non-Accountable Forms and Medical, Dental and Laboratory Supplies and reconcile the balance to the Accounting Office for adjustment;
The Inventory Committee to submit the RPCI and henceforth prepare and submit yearly to the Auditor a duly certified and approved RPCI not later than January 31 of each year; and
The Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting entry if there’s any to reflect the actual balance of Inventories as at December 31, 2023 for fair presentation of the financial statements.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to reconcile records, prepare the necessary adjusting entries, and strictly comply with the provisions of Paragraph 27 of IPSAS 1 to ensure the accuracy and reliability of the account presented in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management require:
The Inventory Committee to conduct an actual physical count on all PPE and submit a duly certified and approved RPCPPE for all types of PPE not later than January 31 of each year;
The Municipal Treasurer and Accountant to maintain Property Cards and  Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards respectively and observe proper maintenance and regular monitoring thereof; and
The Accounting and Property Office to analyze and reconcile the accounting records and property records as soon as the RPCPPE are prepared for a fair presentation of the financial statements.

We recommended that Management:
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to record the required depreciation expenses (closed to Prior Period Adjustment) and Accumulated Depreciation-Other Infrastructure in the amount of ₱822,677.13; and
Propose Adjusting Entry
Prior period Adjustment		822,677.13
Accumulated Depreciation			822,677.13
Strictly adhere to the provisions of Section 50 of the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) for Local Government Units (LGUs) for proper presentation of accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management strictly adhere to the provisions of the New Government Accounting System for LGUs regarding the procedures of accounting for and managing supplies. Accordingly, procured supplies and materials must be recorded as inventories upon purchase and the corresponding expense accounts must be taken up only upon issuance/s thereof.

We recommended that Management strictly comply with the provisions of RA 8424, otherwise known as the National Internal Revenue Code and its implementing regulations, as amended by RA 10963 to help the government in generating revenues to meet its appropriation requirements and to avoid penalties and legal action.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant make the necessary adjustments so as to arrive at the correct balances of the affected accounts in the financial statements and submit proof of remittances to BIR, if there’s any.

We recommended that Management submit justification for not posting the RFQ of those contracts with an ABC above ₱50,000.00 in the Philippine Government Electronic Procurement System (PhilGEPS) website, the website of the Procuring Entity, and at any conspicuous place for further evaluation. Correspondingly, in their succeeding procurement activities, strictly implement the provisions of Annex H Item E of RA 9184 regarding the Advertisement and Posting of Procurement Opportunity.

We recommended that Management submit all the required supporting documents for the payment of honoraria to the members of the BAC and TWG to avoid suspension in audit and, henceforth, strictly adhere to the provisions of Section 4 (6) of Presidential Decree No. 1445 and Section 5.7.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001, thereby ascertain that all claims are regular, proper, and supporting documents are complete.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Engineer to include in the preparation of GPPA those awarded infrastructure contracts but not yet started as of report date and submit the same to the Audit Team within the reglementary period; and
Strictly adhere to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013 to promote transparency and accountability.

We recommended that Management:
Instruct the BAC to check and verify the Purchase requests and Requests for Quotations whether specifications for the procurement of goods are based on relevant characteristics and/or performance requirement or with reference to brand names, if so, return the PR/RFQ to the Office/personnel who prepared them for correction and modification; and
Stop the practice of referring to brand names and strictly comply with the provisions of RA 9184 and its IRR in all procurement activities to promote competitiveness in government procurement.

We recommended that Management enumerate specifically the Priority Infrastructure Projects in its Annual Budget to facilitate the evaluation of the projects charged against the 20% Development Fund. Strict adherence to the provisions DBM-DOF-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1 dated November 4, 2020 is enjoined.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the GFPS TWG:
Reinforce the preparation of the GAD Plan and Budget with a more prudent gender analysis through establishing the cause/s of identified gender issues and thereafter formulate the PPA/s that directly address the same; and
Identify and address the causes of delay of implementation of the PPAs identified in its Annual GAD Plan.

We recommended the Management to direct the Treasurer’s Office personnel to conduct intensive tax campaign and employ collection strategies, giving priority to the collection of the Real Property Tax and Special Education Tax Receivables which had accumulated to ₱38.103 million and prevent the balance of the accounts from accumulating further. We also recommended that Notice of Delinquency or final demand letters be sent to the delinquent owners and avail remedies through levy on real property or judicial actions provided under RA 7160.

We recommended that Management ensure to enter into Contract of Lease/MOAs to all occupants of the stalls in the market. We also recommended that Management strictly imposed the provisions in the contract regarding non-payment of rentals by the lease owners and institute measures to collect the arrears.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant effect the corrections in the Notes to Financial Statements (FS) for the period-ended December 31, 2023 as detailed and presented in the Revisions/Remarks column above in order to enhance the understandability and reliability of the financial statements.

Otherwise, withhold the salaries of the responsible persons if warranted.

We recommended that Management exert all efforts to verify the details of the account and reconcile with the Audit Team. We also recommended that Management exhaust all available remedies in enforcing the immediate settlement of all audit disallowances pursuant to pertinent provisions of COA Circular No. 2009-006 and to be more prudent in the processing of claims for payments to avoid audit disallowances and suspensions.

We commended the Management for the utilization of the funds received from the NGAs in compliance with the above-cited regulations. Improper Charges to 5% LDRRM Fund
Ineligible expenditures amounting to ₱0.261 million were charged against the LDRRM Fund contrary to Section 4.3 of NDRRMC-DILG- DBM-CSC Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2014-01, Section 5 of NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 and COA Circular No. 2012-002, thus decreasing the LDRRMF by the same amount that could be used for more vital disaster activities.
Section 4.3 of the NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC JMC No. 2014-01 dated April 4, 2014 states that:
“the budgetary requirements for personal services, maintenance and other operating expenditures, and capital outlay of the LDRRMO shall be sourced from the General Fund of the LGU, subject to Section 76 of RA 7160.”
Additionally, Section 5 of the NDRRMC, DBM and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013- l dated March 25, 2013 enumerated the projects/activities that could be undertaken in the utilization of the LDRRMF.
Lastly, COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 on the Accounting and Reporting Guidelines for the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund (LDRRMF) provides that:
3.0 Purposes of the LDRRMF
The LDRRMF shall be used to support disaster risk management activities such as, but not limited to the following:
3.1 Pre-disaster preparedness programs such as training of personnel, and purchase of life saving and rescue equipment, and supplies and medicines;
3.2 Post-disaster activities such as repair and rehabilitation of public infrastructures and purchase of office/school equipment damaged by calamities during the budget year;
3.3 Payment of insurance premiums on property if indemnity includes damages or loss due to fire, earthquake, storm or other casualties and on the personnel accident insurance of Accredited Community Disaster Volunteers; and
3.4 Relief and recovery programs in communities or areas stricken by disasters, calamities, epidemics or complex emergencies.
Review of documents relative to the utilization of the LDRRM Fund revealed that a portion of the 70% Mitigation fund was utilized for PPAs which are not supportive of disaster risk management activities. Among those requisitioned were overtime pay, maintenance and operating expenses and capital outlay of the LDRRM Office, as follows:
The above-mentioned expenditures were not appropriate charges against the LDRRM Fund and should have been charged against the General Fund (GF) of the LGU, as stated in Section 4.3 of the NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC JMC No. 2014-01. Also, these PPAs do not fall in any of the eligible PPAs stated in the above regulations.
The Management’s deviation from the said regulations resulted in diminishing the LDRRM Fund by ₱261,735.26, which could have been used for more vital disaster activities and/or eligible PPAs.
We recommended that Management, through the Municipal DRRM Officer, to utilize the LDRRMF strictly for implementation of projects and activities exclusively for disaster prevention, mitigation, preparedness, response, rehabilitation and recovery as envisioned in NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC JMC No. 2014-01, Section 5 of NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 and COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended the Management:
a. through the Municipal Accountant to conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis, and validation of the existence of the receivables and determine the concerned debtors to ascertain their collectability. If proven to be no longer collectible, request for authority to write-off dormant receivable accounts and attach required documents prescribed under COA Circular No. 2016-005; and
b. in the future, for transactions involving receivables, the Municipal Accountant should maintain subsidiary ledgers in accordance with Section 4 (e) of the NGAS to ensure complete information on the accounts and to facilitate the collection thereof.

We recommended the Management to:
a. continuously require/demand the immediate settlement of the remaining overdue cash advances and if necessary, withhold the salaries of the concerned officials and employees for the settlement thereof; and
b. strictly comply with the provisions of Section 89 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 97 – 002 dated February 10, 1997 in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to trace the original entry made and make the necessary adjustments on the outstanding payable accounts and ensure that such claims are properly documented and supported with valid claims of speciﬁc creditors, processed vouchers or payrolls, demand payments or bills. Likewise, the Accounting Unit should maintain complete and updated subsidiary ledgers for Payable accounts. The payable accounts that has been outstanding for more than 2 years or more with no documents evidencing valid claims should be reverted to Unappropriated Surplus of the General Fund.

We reiterated our recommendation for Management, through the Property Officer to require each offices to prepare and submit their Monthly Report of Fuel Consumptions for consolidation and  for the driver of each of the motor vehicle of the LGU to prepare at the end of the month, a Monthly Report of Official Travels, summarizing in chronological order his trips for the month which shall be certified by him and approved by the LCE or his/her authorized representative to ensure that fuel consumption is effectively monitored and used only for official businesses in conformity with COA Circular 77-61.

We commended Management for providing at least 5 percent of the total budget appropriations for GAD PPAs. Nevertheless, we recommended that Management comply with the above-mentioned law and regulation by implementing PPAs which are gender-responsive and could address gender issues and concerns. Likewise, specific gender issue or concern should be identified for a particular GAD PPA and this must be stated in the GAD Plan and Budget and Accomplishment Report.

We recommended that Management stop the practice of allowing reimbursements to officials and employees and enforce direct payment to suppliers or creditors as prescribed under Section 93 of PD 1445 and RA9184.

We recommended and the Management agreed to instruct the MDRRMO, to revisit the guidelines relative to the utilization of the LDRRMF to ensure and enforce the strict compliance thereof. Moreover, identify the projects, programs and activities, by assessing which to prioritize and implement vis-à-vis the LGU determined disaster related issues/PPA’s, after due consultation with the stakeholders. Finally, for management to involve all concerned officials to improve and institutionalize the processes on Planning, Execution, Procurement and Recording as regard Disaster Risk Reduction and Management, to address the needs of LGU constituents and attain resilience, as envisioned in R.A, No. 10121.

We recommended and the Management agreed to refrain from accommodating expenditures which do not fall within the prescribed programs, activities and projects chargeable against the LDRRMF, and to strictly adhere with the guidelines, rules and regulations on the proper utilization/disbursement of the LDRRM Fund.

We recommended and the Management agreed through the Municipal Accountant to:
• strengthen the controls being adopted pertaining to the proper identification and recording of transactions by diligently reviewing the nature and purpose of each transaction in order to record them in the appropriate account and reflect their true and correct balances; and
• draw the following adjusting entries, to reflect the proper classification of transactions to their proper accounts.
For salaries and wages of COS employees charged in other general services
Janitorial Services ₱234,240.00
Other General Services ₱234,240.00
For the transfer of unexpended balance of LDRRM fund to Special Trust Fund
General Fund Books
Subsidy to Other Funds ₱231,418.36
Cash in Bank ₱231,418.36
Trust Fund Books
Cash in Bank – Trust Fund ₱231,418.36
Trust Liability – DRRMF ₱231,418.36

We recommended and the Management agreed to direct the Municipal Development Council and Municipal Planning and Development Office, to ensure that the Approved Annual Investment Plan and Annual Budget for the appropriation of the 20% Development Fund shall contain Local Development Plan, supported by listing/s of specific programs, projects and activities to be undertaken to facilitate proper evaluation, assessment and evaluation, pursuant to existing laws, rules and regulations.

We recommended and the Management agreed to strictly monitor the utilization of 20% DF by ensuring that disbursements are charge directly to its related and approved PPAs, and comply with the procedural processes provided under Section 321 of RA 7160 and other existing laws and regulations relative to realignment of funds.

We recommended and the Management agreed, through the Municipal Accounting Office to submit immediately the lacking documents mentioned above, to support the reported disbursements, and strengthen the monitoring and control on expenditures by ensuring that documents are complete and sufficient before processing for payment, to establish validity and propriety of the transactions, and avoid possible audit suspensions/disallowances.

We recommended and the Management agreed to refrain from the payment of Mid-year and other bonus earlier than the schedule/timeline provided under existing rules and regulations, in order not to deprived the constituents from the benefits of the specific program, projects and activities to be funded therefrom.

We recommended and the Management agreed to intensify control measures in the recording and custodianship of Property Plant and Equipment (PPE), to produce accurate and reliable presentation of PPE accounts in the financial statements totaling ₱45,764,839.68 as of December 31, 2023, and specifically addressing the following:
• Assign additional personnel that will help the Treasurer in carrying out its function as concurrent Property Officer and create an Inventory Committee that will perform the functions attached therein.
• Require the accounting office to record semi-expendable items in the respective inventory/expense accounts
• Issue the corresponding Inventory Custodian Slip (ICS) to all users of the semi- expendable items.
• Include all necessary, relevant and complete information regarding the advance payment for the purchase of land in the notes to financial statements.

We recommended and the Management agreed, with the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer as lead, strengthen cash controls in accounting and recording, to exact accountability and accuracy of CIB-LCCA by:
• advising the Accounting Office to prepare and submit the BRS within the prescribed period or at least within a reasonable time and formally documenting in writing the cash and reconciliation standards or policies and key controls are in place and effective in addressing the concerns affecting the accounting and recording of CIB-LCCA for the complete, timely and accurate reporting; and
• establishing a practicable and effective control activities by the Treasurer’s Office to facilitate the reconciliation of cash balances with that of the accounting office.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Engineer to furnish the AT list of all on-going government PPAs and those that are to be implemented during the year as mandated on the aforementioned regulation of law in order that the AT could conduct timely validation and monitoring of the implementation of infrastructure projects, thus any deficiencies will be promptly informed to management for immediately and appropriate action thereon.

Discontinue granting benefits to its employees without a sufficient and valid legal basis.

The late implementation of Salary Standardization Law (SSL);
 To sustain the increasing needs of employees in the midst of high inflation brought about by the worldwide impact of the coronavirus crisis; and
In lieu of other benefits supposedly enjoyed by regular employees such as medical/ medicine allowances, relocation allowance, collective negotiation agreement (CNA) and the like.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following corrective measures for the aforesaid deficiencies:

Strict compliance on the documentary requirements under COA Circular 2012-001.

Strictly comply Sec. 22. Only employees with accumulated 15 days VL should be allowed to monetize. Make sure that at least five (5) days balance is retained after monetization.

In case the employees wish to monetize 50% or more of their accumulated leave credits (VL & SL) require them to submit justifiable reasons and additional requirements as provided for under Sec.23.

The HRMO and Municipal Accountant should closely monitor leave credit balances of the employees, maintain and updated leave cards of each employee.

 Stop the practice of allowing employees to monetize more than once during the year.
observations.managementAction:
Management has complied with the documentary requirements.




Management has started the application of the audit recommendation to ensure that at least five (5) days balance is retained after monetization. 




Management has already informed tits officials and employees on submission of justifiable reasons and additional requirements in monetization of 50% or more of their accumulated leave credits.




The HRMO and Municipal Accountant has complied with the recommendation.








Management has complied with the recommendation.

We reiterated our recommendations that:

The Municipal Accountant take responsibility to conduct a final review, analysis and reconciliation of all dormant accounts pursuant to COA Circular 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 on the Guidelines and Procedures on the Write-off of Dormant Receivable Accounts, Unliquidated Cash Advances, and Fund Transfers to National Government Agencies (NGAs), Local Government Units (LGUs) and Government-Owned and Controlled Corporations (GOCCs); and 

If the analysis/review of the accounts/funds is not possible due to the absence of records and documents, the local chief executive should request to the Commission on Audit for write-off and/or adjustment of account balances supported by list of available records and extent of validation made on these dormant accounts and Certification and reasons why the books of accounts/records, financial documents and schedules cannot be located.
observations.managementAction: No management action.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

require the GSO to maintain a complete, accurate and updated Property Cards (PCs) based on records and reconcile the same regularly with the Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards (PPELCs) maintained by the Accounting Office;

require the GSO and the Municipal Accountant to effect the necessary adjustments in their records, in order to reflect the correct balance of every PPE account; and


require the GSO to submit a duly accomplished/filled-out RPCPPE to the Auditor not later than January 31 of the ensuing year.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended Management to:

Strictly adhere with the provisions of Section 29, Volume 1 of the MNGAS for LGUs

Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to closely supervise and monitor the LO and ensure that the latter in return monitor the collectors on a daily basis and see to it that their collections are promptly remitted;

Any unissued ORs on the hands of the collectors are verified and see to it that all collections corresponding to the used ORs are remitted intact to the Municipal Treasurer.

Thus, possible loss, personal use/misuse of the collections will be avoided. In addition, this will also enable the Municipality to earn additional income in the form of interest on bank deposits and the timely use of funds for the implementation of its projects.
observations.managementAction:
Report of Collection and Deposit is done on weekly basis.

Collectors remitted their collections on a weekly basis.

The Municipal Treasurer verified the unissued Official Receipts.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Observation No. 2 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management advise the Municipal Treasurer to enforce a more vigorous and effective tax campaign, employ a realistic and effective collection strategies, and institute legal remedies pursuant to pertinent provisions of RA 7160 in order to persuade the taxpayers to pay their taxes on time and to attain one hundred per cent 100% collection efficiency so that more programs, projects and activities can be implemented for the benefit of the Municipality’s constituents.

We reiterated our recommendation that:

the Municipal Accountant take responsibility to conduct a final review, analysis and reconciliation of all dormant accounts pursuant to COA Circular 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 on the Guidelines and Procedures on the Write-off of Dormant Receivable Accounts, Unliquidated Cash Advances, and Fund Transfers to National Government Agencies (NGAs), Local Government Units (LGUs) and Government-Owned and Controlled Corporations (GOCCs); and
if the analysis/review of the accounts/funds is not possible due to the absence of records and documents, the local chief executive should request to the Commission on Audit for write-off and/or adjustment of account balances supported by list of available records and extent of validation made on these dormant accounts and certification and reasons why the books of accounts/records, financial documents and schedules cannot be located.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Management return/refund immediately the unutilized balances of the fund transfers to the source National Government Agencies to fully liquidate the same, make necessary adjustments to the liability account of the LGU and to avoid the existence of idle funds which could be utilized by the Government for other development projects, programs and activities.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive advise the Municipal Accountant to exercise due diligence in gathering data/details of the long outstanding account Due to LGUs amounting to ₱1,682,202.71 to determine the validity and legality to warrant reversion of the accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant disclose in the CY 2022 and succeeding years Notes to Financial Statements the details on the receipt and utilization of LDRRMF of the Municipality using the format as required under Item 5.1.16 of COA Circular 2012-002.

We recommended that the Management to ensure that all development projects are well-planned and procurement and implementation ready in order to complete them within the target completion date to maximize the benefit for its intended beneficiaries. We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive advise the concerned personnel to furnish the Office of the Auditor a copy of the quarterly reports on the utilization of the 20% Development Fund, consistent with the reporting requirements prescribed by the DOF-BLGF.

We recommended that the Management thru the Local Chief Executive the following courses of action:

Require the Municipal Development Council to specify the details of the identified priority projects to be charged under the 20% Development Fund including project location, specific implementation schedule, and physical target to avoid generic or lumpsum projects.
Stop the practice of implementing generic or lumpsum projects without further authority from the Sanggunian Bayan to avoid suspension/disallowance in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer direct the preparer of checks to be cautious in entering data in the face of the checks and device a control measure to avoid the frequent error and waste of government resources.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Treasurer, being the head of Treasury Office, and the Liquidating Officer to deposit all collections as required by the rules and regulations to prevent misappropriation, theft and loss of funds. Likewise, proper handling of collections and its deposits be strictly observed and deposits should be attributed/traced from the collected amounts. Impose sanctions, if warranted.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant:

conduct regular reconciliation of cashbooks, records and reports at the end of each month or at least quarterly prior to the preparation of financial reports to avoid accumulation of unreconciled variances; and
effect the necessary adjustments in the reconciling items to reflect the adjusted balances of affected books and fund accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
create an Appraisal and Disposal Committee which will undertake the necessary action/s for the immediate disposal of the unserviceable properties to avoid further deterioration and consequent depreciation in its value. A systematic and timely disposal will yield benefits of, among others, a higher appraised value, enabling management storage areas available for other purposes and relieving the Supply Officer of unnecessary accountability.
direct the Municipal Accountant to derecognize the unserviceable properties upon disposal as prescribed under Paragraph 82, Volume I of IPSAS and Section 4.3 of Commission on Audit (COA) Circular No. 2020-006 to ensure that the PPE accounts are fairly presented in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to:
closely supervise and monitor the collectors and ensure that the latter promptly remit their collection on a daily basis as required by Section 29, Volume 1 of the MNGAS for LGUs
deposit the collections intact daily or as soon as it reaches the threshold as prescribed by Section 32, Volume I of the MNGAS for LGUs and Section 28 of COA Circular No. 92-382 dated July 3, 1992 in order to avoid possible loss, personal use or misuse of the undeposited collection and enables the Municipality to earn interest that could accrue from these collections.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to maintain special account under the General Fund for the operation of the public market, slaughter house and hospital and prepare separate financial reports which provide information about the profitability and operation of these that can serve as a guide to come up with better economic decisions by the management and to have ready information necessary for the interested parties and the general public.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer to stop the practice of paying expenses on a reimbursement basis. As far as practicable, disbursements shall be paid directly to the suppliers/dealers and by means of checks as provided in the above provision of P.D. 1445. We further recommend that the Municipal Government look into alternative suppliers who are willing to accept purchase orders from the municipality to ensure that all gasoline expenses are paid directly to the suppliers.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Engineer to furnish the AT list of all on-going government PPAs and those that are to be implemented during the year as mandated on the aforementioned regulation of law in order that the AT could conduct timely validation and monitoring of the implementation of infrastructure projects, thus any deficiencies will be promptly informed to management for immediately and appropriate action thereon.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the BAC to strictly comply with the time frame specified in Section 20, Rule VII of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 in the conduct of Pre-Procurement Conferences. This is to ensure that the conference serves its intended purpose and prevents potential issues or delays during the procurement process and achieve the most efficient and effective procurement outcome.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the BAC to comply with the following:
issue a Supplemental/Bid Bulletin in compliance with Section 38.2 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 for purposes of transparency.
post the supplemental/bid bulletin in the PhilGEPS website as required by Section 22.5.3 of Revised IRR of RA 9184 and to the website of the Procuring Entity concerned, if available, and at any conspicuous place within the premises of the Procuring Entity, thus fairness and transparency of the procurement process could be attained.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct all requisitioning Department Heads to submit a duly accomplished/filled out PR to the BAC and the latter to verify and ensure that the same are properly accomplished as to specifications, quantity, ABC, and other terms and conditions of the item to be procured of which these are also clearly stated in the RFQ. Thus, the Bidders could accurately and competitively give corresponding quotations that are most advantageous to the municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Bids and Awards Committee to adhere strictly to the provisions prescribed by Section 18, Rule VI of the Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA 9184. Thus, promote a more competitive pricing structure and ensure a fair and impartial evaluation during the procurement process.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the BAC to see to it that the provisions set forth under Annex H, Appendix A of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 are strictly followed before the perfection of the contract/purchase order to ensure the completeness of the required supporting documents.  Also, require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that the required necessary supporting documents are complete prior to the payment of the transactions, thus validity, legality and propriety of the transactions could be ascertained.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) instruct the Municipal Engineer to require the contractors to submit all documents supporting the infrastructure projects and the Municipal Accountant to see to it that all disbursement vouchers are duly supported with complete documentation in compliance with Section 4 (6) of Presidential Decree No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 in order to substantiate the legality, validity and propriety of transactions and in order to avoid the issuance of NS.
Likewise, for the aforementioned 14 projects, we recommend that the LCE require that Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the Municipal Engineer to facilitate the immediate submission of the lacking supporting documents for audit and inspection purposes, after which, any deficiencies will be communicated to management for immediately and appropriate action thereon.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Bids and Awards Committee and the Municipal Engineer to coordinate with each other and initiate the conduct of immediate public bidding of the Construction/Improvement of Lamag Multipurpose Building to provide the corresponding benefits to the end-users/farmers.

We recommended that the Management justify/explain the incurrence of cash overdraft on the Trust Fund of the Municipality. Likewise, we recommended that the prompt remittance of the unutilized trust funds due to other government agencies to avoid misappropriation thereof.
observations.managementAction: The reason on the insufficient cash availability was due to dormant trust fund account which was not moving since 1997 that includes receivables from NGAs and other receivables totaling ₱1,331,845.73 which was due for write-off.

observations.recommendation:

Office of the Municipal Treasurer explain/justify the late submission of the supporting documents of the amount disbursed under Check Number 846004 dated July 1, 2021 amounting to ₱5,710.00 to the Office of the Municipal Accountant.;

Municipal Treasurer submit immediately to the Office of the Municipal Accountant the disbursement voucher and its supporting documents on the reimbursement of PNP for COVID-19 related expenses amounting to ₱5,710.00 for proper recording in the books of accounts; and

Municipal Treasurer notify the payees of the six (6) staled checks to return the checks under their possession prior to the issuance of a new check.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Management advise the Municipal Accountant to send Letters of Demand to Aranaar ARB Multi-Purpose Cooperative and Quirino Market Vendors informing them the immediate payment of their loans, copy furnished the Office of the Auditor, and record or disclose in the books of accounts the accumulated penalties on delayed payments.
observations.managementAction: The Management sent letters of demand to creditors, however, due to COVID-19 pandemic, the debtors were not able to pay their loans and they asked for condonation of their loans.

observations.recommendation: We commended the collective efforts of the Municipal officials and employees on the effective and efficient disbursement of COVID19 funds. Likewise, we recommended that the Management continue their control mechanism towards the spread of COVID19 virus.
observations.managementAction: The Management continue their efforts to mitigate the spread of COVID-19 virus which resulted into zero COVID-19 patient as of December 31, 2022.
The Management submitted the documentary requirements and implemented the recommendations of the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation:
We appreciated that the Municipality of Quirino had passed the required procedural due process for their reprogramming on the above subject projects, however, we recommended that the Management:

Formulate more realistic socio-economic and environmental programs and projects and prioritize their implementation according to the needs of the constituents to avoid constant reprogramming and reversion;

Focus on those projects and activities proposed or recommended by the members of the Municipal Planning and Development Council that are endemic to their area of jurisdiction that will fit the genuine capability of the Municipality to undertake such projects directed to yield and precise Annual Investment Plan; and 

Require the Executive Committee to ensure that the decisions of the council are faithfully carried out and implemented.
observations.managementAction: The Management complied with our audit recommendations.

observations.recommendation:

Refrain from appropriating funds to unrelated projects and activities of disaster risk reduction and management and adhere to the provisions of the NDRRMC, DBM and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013;

Revise the LDRRMP and AIP for CY 2022 to reprogram the above mentioned PPAs to disaster preparedness related projects, programs and activities and submit to the Auditor’s Office the copy of the revised LDRRMP and AIP for CY 2022; and

Explain/ justify why the above mentioned PPAs were funded under the LDRRMF to forestall the issuance of Notice of Disallowance on the amount disbursed for CY 2021.
